narrative: Here Adeimantus interposed a question: "How would you answer, Socrates," said he, "if a person were to say that you are making these people miserable, and that they are the cause of their own unhappiness; the city in fact belongs to them, but they are none the better for it; whereas other men acquire lands, and build large and handsome houses, and have everything handsome about them, offering sacrifices to the gods on their own account, and practising hospitality; moreover, as you were saying just now, they have gold and silver, and all that is usual among the favourites of fortune; but our poor citizens are no better than mercenaries who are quartered in the city and are always mounting guard?"
refashioned: At this point, Adeimantus jumped in with a question: "How would you respond, Socrates, if someone said you're making these guardians miserable -- that they're the cause of their own unhappiness? The city belongs to them, yet they get nothing out of it. Other people acquire land, build grand houses, furnish them beautifully, make sacrifices to the gods, and entertain guests. As you yourself just pointed out, they have gold and silver and all the trappings of good fortune. But our citizens? They're nothing more than hired soldiers garrisoned in the city, perpetually standing watch."
elegant: Adeimantus raises an objection: aren't we making the guardians miserable? The city belongs to them, yet they gain nothing from it. Other men acquire property, live in luxury, and enjoy every advantage of wealth. Our guardians are no better than mercenaries permanently stationed on guard duty.
summary: Adeimantus challenges Socrates by asking how he would respond to the charge that the guardians are being made miserable -- they own the city but enjoy none of its benefits, living like mercenaries while others accumulate wealth and luxury.
distilled: Adeimantus objects that the guardians, despite being the city's protectors, live like impoverished mercenaries while others enjoy wealth and comfort.
notes: Speakers: Adeimantus poses the question, Socrates will respond -- Adeimantus is Plato's brother, along with Glaucon -- This objection follows directly from Book III's establishment of the guardians' austere communal lifestyle -- The mercenary comparison anticipates the distinction between true guardians and mere soldiers -- Cross-ref: Book III 416d-417b on the prohibition of private property for guardians -- The concern about guardian happiness recurs in Book VII when discussing the philosopher's reluctant return to the cave

narrative:
"Yes," I said; "and you may add that they are only fed, and not paid in addition to their food, like other men; and therefore they cannot, if they would, take a journey of pleasure; they have no money to spend on a mistress or any other luxurious fancy, which, as the world goes, is thought to be happiness; and many other accusations of the same nature might be added."

"But," said he, "let us suppose all this to be included in the charge."
refashioned:
"Yes," I said, "and you could add that they're only given food -- no salary on top of it, like other men receive. So even if they wanted to, they couldn't take a vacation. They have no money to spend on a lover or any other indulgence that the world considers happiness. And plenty of similar accusations could be piled on."

"Well," he said, "let's assume all of that is included in the charge."
elegant: Socrates concedes the objection can be pushed even further: the guardians receive only food, no wages. They cannot travel for pleasure or spend on luxuries the world considers happiness. Adeimantus agrees to include all such charges.
summary: Socrates actually strengthens Adeimantus's objection by noting that the guardians receive only food with no pay, cannot travel for pleasure, and have no money for any indulgence the world considers happiness. Adeimantus accepts all this as part of the charge.
distilled: Socrates amplifies the objection -- guardians receive only food, no wages, and cannot partake in any pleasure the world associates with happiness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates amplifies the charge, Adeimantus accepts it -- Socrates' rhetorical strategy of strengthening the opponent's case before answering is characteristic of his dialectical method -- The list of pleasures (travel, mistresses, luxury) represents conventional Greek happiness (eudaimonia) -- Cross-ref: This anticipates the fuller treatment of pleasure in Book IX -- The word 'happiness' here reflects the common understanding Plato will challenge

narrative:
"You mean to ask," I said, "what will be our answer?"

"Yes."

"If we proceed along the old path, my belief," I said, "is that we shall find the answer. And our answer will be that, even as they are, our guardians may very likely be the happiest of men; but that our aim in founding the State was not the disproportionate happiness of any one class, but the greatest happiness of the whole; we thought that in a State which is ordered with a view to the good of the whole we should be most likely to find Justice, and in the ill-ordered State injustice: and, having found them, we might then decide which of the two is the happier. At present, I take it, we are fashioning the happy State, not piecemeal, or with a view of making a few happy citizens, but as a whole; and by-and-by we will proceed to view the opposite kind of State.

Suppose that we were painting a statue, and some one came up to us and said, 'Why do you not put the most beautiful colours on the most beautiful parts of the body -- the eyes ought to be purple, but you have made them black' -- to him we might fairly answer, 'Sir, you would not surely have us beautify the eyes to such a degree that they are no longer eyes; consider rather whether, by giving this and the other features their due proportion, we make the whole beautiful.'

And so I say to you, do not compel us to assign to the guardians a sort of happiness which will make them anything but guardians; for we too can clothe our husbandmen in royal apparel, and set crowns of gold on their heads, and bid them till the ground as much as they like, and no more. Our potters also might be allowed to repose on couches, and feast by the fireside, passing round the winecup, while their wheel is conveniently at hand, and working at pottery only as much as they like; in this way we might make every class happy -- and then, as you imagine, the whole State would be happy."
refashioned:
"You want to know how we'd answer that?" I said.

"Yes."

"If we stay on the path we've been following, I believe we'll find the answer. Our guardians, even as they are, may well be the happiest of men. But our goal in founding the city was never the lopsided happiness of any single class -- it was the greatest happiness of the whole. We believed that in a city ordered for the good of everyone, we'd be most likely to find justice, and in a disordered city, injustice. Having found them, we could then judge which produces more happiness. Right now, we're designing the happy city as a whole -- not piecemeal, not to make a few citizens happy at the expense of others.

Imagine we were painting a statue, and someone came up and said, 'Why haven't you used the most beautiful colors on the most beautiful parts? The eyes should be purple, but you've made them black.' We'd rightly answer: 'Surely you wouldn't have us beautify the eyes to the point where they're no longer recognizable as eyes. Consider instead whether, by giving each feature its proper proportion, we make the whole thing beautiful.'

So I say to you: don't force us to give the guardians a kind of happiness that would make them anything but guardians. We could just as easily dress our farmers in royal robes, crown them with gold, and tell them to farm only when they feel like it. Our potters could lounge on couches, feast by the fire, pass around the wine, keep the wheel handy, and work only as much as they please. We could make every class 'happy' this way -- and then, as you imagine, the whole city would be happy."
elegant: The aim in founding the city was not the disproportionate happiness of one class, but the greatest happiness of the whole. In a city ordered for the common good, justice is most likely to be found. Just as a painter would not use the most beautiful colors on the eyes if it made them cease to look like eyes -- each part must have its proper proportion for the whole to be beautiful. Give every class self-indulgent happiness and none will perform its function; the city will collapse.
summary: Socrates answers that the city was founded not for the happiness of any single class but for the greatest happiness of the whole. He uses the analogy of painting a statue: you don't make the eyes the most beautiful color if it ruins the whole. Making every class indulgently happy would destroy each class's function and the city itself.
distilled: The city's aim is the happiness of the whole, not any single class; each part must fulfill its proper function for the whole to flourish.
notes: Speakers: Socrates delivers the core response to Adeimantus's challenge -- The statue-painting analogy is one of Plato's most famous images for organic unity -- 'Greatest happiness of the whole' is sometimes read as proto-utilitarian, but Plato's meaning is structural, not aggregative -- Cross-ref: The organic unity principle returns in Book VIII when describing how cities degenerate -- The farmer and potter examples show how indulging any class destroys its function -- Cross-ref: The principle of specialization established in Book II (369b-370c)

narrative:
"But do not put this idea into our heads; for, if we listen to you, the husbandman will be no longer a husbandman, the potter will cease to be a potter, and no one will have the character of any distinct class in the State. Now this is not of much consequence where the corruption of society, and pretension to be what you are not, is confined to cobblers; but when the guardians of the laws and of the government are only seemingly and not real guardians, then see how they turn the State upside down; and on the other hand they alone have the power of giving order and happiness to the State.

We mean our guardians to be true saviours and not the destroyers of the State, whereas our opponent is thinking of peasants at a festival, who are enjoying a life of revelry, not of citizens who are doing their duty to the State. But, if so, we mean different things, and he is speaking of something which is not a State. And therefore we must consider whether in appointing our guardians we would look to their greatest happiness individually, or whether this principle of happiness does not rather reside in the State as a whole."
refashioned:
"But don't let this line of thinking take hold -- because if we listen to it, the farmer will stop being a farmer, the potter will stop being a potter, and no class in the city will retain its distinct character. Now, when this kind of corruption -- pretending to be what you're not -- is limited to cobblers, it doesn't matter much. But when the guardians of the laws and government are only pretending to be guardians, watch how they turn the whole city upside down. And yet, they alone have the power to bring the city order and happiness.

We intend our guardians to be the city's true protectors, not its destroyers. Our critic is imagining peasants at a festival, enjoying a life of revelry -- not citizens performing their duty. If that's his vision, we're talking about completely different things. What he's describing isn't a city at all. So the real question is: in appointing our guardians, should we aim at their individual happiness, or does true happiness reside in the city as a whole?"
elegant: If every class abandons its function for pleasure, the city loses its structure. When corruption is confined to cobblers, it matters little -- but when guardians become merely apparent guardians, they destroy the city. True guardians are protectors, not revelers. The question is whether happiness belongs to individual classes or to the whole.
summary: Socrates warns that if classes abandon their roles for pleasure, the city's structure collapses. False guardians are especially dangerous since they alone have the power to bring order or ruin. The real question is whether happiness resides in individual classes or in the city as a whole.
distilled: When guardians abandon their true function for personal happiness, they become the city's destroyers rather than its protectors.
notes: Speakers: Socrates continues his response to Adeimantus -- The distinction between real and apparent guardians is crucial -- it foreshadows the philosopher-king argument in Books V-VII -- 'Peasants at a festival' contrasts leisure-as-happiness with duty-as-happiness -- Cross-ref: Book III's discussion of guardian education -- The cobbler analogy anticipates the justice argument: meddling across class boundaries is the definition of injustice (4.36-4.37)

narrative:
"But the latter be the truth, then the guardians and auxiliaries, and all others equally with them, must be compelled or induced to do their own work in the best way. And thus the whole State will grow up in a noble order, and the several classes will receive the proportion of happiness which nature assigns to them."

"I think that you are quite right."

"I wonder whether you will agree with another remark which occurs to me."

"What may that be?"

"There seem to be two causes of the deterioration of the arts."

"What are they?"

"Wealth," I said, "and poverty."

"How do they act?"

"The process is as follows: When a potter becomes rich, will he, think you, any longer take the same pains with his art?"

"Certainly not."

"He will grow more and more indolent and careless?"

"Very true."

"And the result will be that he becomes a worse potter?"

"Yes; he greatly deteriorates."

"But, on the other hand, if he has no money, and cannot provide himself tools or instruments, he will not work equally well himself, nor will he teach his sons or apprentices to work equally well."

"Certainly not."

"Then, under the influence either of poverty or of wealth, workmen and their work are equally liable to degenerate?"

"That is evident."

"Here, then, is a discovery of new evils," I said, "against which the guardians will have to watch, or they will creep into the city unobserved."

"What evils?"

"Wealth," I said, "and poverty; the one is the parent of luxury and indolence, and the other of meanness and viciousness, and both of discontent."

"That is very true," he replied; "but still I should like to know, Socrates, how our city will be able to go to war, especially against an enemy who is rich and powerful, if deprived of the sinews of war."

"There would certainly be a difficulty," I replied, "in going to war with one such enemy; but there is no difficulty where there are two of them."

"How so?" he asked.

"In the first place," I said, "if we have to fight, our side will be trained warriors fighting against an army of rich men."
refashioned:
"If this is true, then the guardians, auxiliaries, and everyone else must be compelled or encouraged to do their own work as well as possible. This way, the whole city will develop in proper order, and each class will receive the share of happiness that nature assigns to it."

"I think you're exactly right."

"Let me raise another point. There seem to be two causes of decline in any craft."

"What are they?"

"Wealth and poverty."

"How so?"

"Consider: when a potter becomes rich, will he still take the same care with his work?"

"Of course not."

"He'll become lazy and careless?"

"Naturally."

"And he'll become a worse potter?"

"Much worse."

"But if he's too poor to afford proper tools, he won't produce good work either, and he won't be able to train his apprentices properly."

"True."

"So both wealth and poverty cause work to deteriorate?"

"Clearly."

"Here's a new threat the guardians must watch for -- wealth and poverty creeping into the city. Wealth breeds luxury and laziness; poverty breeds desperation and vice. Both breed discontent."

"True enough," Adeimantus replied, "but how will our city go to war against rich, powerful enemies if it lacks the resources of war?"

"Fighting one such enemy would be difficult," I said, "but not if there are two of them."

"How do you mean?"

"If we must fight, our side will be trained warriors facing an army of rich men."
elegant: Each class must do its own work excellently; thus the city grows in proper order and each receives its natural share of happiness. Two forces corrupt every craft: wealth breeds laziness, poverty breeds desperation -- both produce inferior work and discontent. The guardians must guard against both. As for war against wealthy enemies, trained warriors will always outfight rich amateurs.
summary: Each class must fulfill its role for the city to thrive. Both wealth and poverty corrupt craftsmanship -- wealth through laziness, poverty through lack of resources. The guardians must prevent both from creeping in. When Adeimantus worries about fighting wealthy enemies, Socrates argues trained warriors will always defeat rich amateurs.
distilled: Both wealth and poverty corrupt excellence in any craft; the guardians must prevent both extremes to maintain the city's order and strength.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus in rapid dialogue -- The potter example illustrates the corrupting effects of both extremes of wealth -- 'Sinews of war' is a proverbial expression -- The argument that trained warriors beat wealthy amateurs reflects Greek military values and Spartan-style thinking -- Cross-ref: Book II's principle of specialization -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on how wealth-accumulation leads to oligarchic degeneration -- Wealth and poverty as twin evils anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean

narrative:
"That is true," he said.

"And do you not suppose, Adeimantus, that a single boxer who was perfect in his art would easily be a match for two stout and well-to-do gentlemen who were not boxers?"

"Hardly, if they came upon him at once."

"What, not," I said, "if he were able to run away and then turn and strike at the one who first came up? And supposing he were to do this several times under the heat of a scorching sun, might he not, being an expert, overturn more than one stout personage?"

"Certainly," he said, "there would be nothing wonderful in that."

"And yet rich men probably have a greater superiority in the science and practice of boxing than they have in military qualities."

"Likely enough."

"Then we may assume that our athletes will be able to fight with two or three times their own number?"
refashioned:
"True," he said.

"Consider this, Adeimantus: wouldn't a single expert boxer easily handle two well-fed gentlemen who don't know how to fight?"

"Not if they attacked him simultaneously."

"But what if he could retreat and then turn to strike whoever came forward first? If he repeated this tactic under a scorching sun, couldn't an expert take down more than one out-of-shape opponent?"

"Certainly -- there'd be nothing surprising about that."

"And rich men probably have a greater advantage in boxing skill than they do in military training."

"Probably so."

"Then we can assume our trained warriors could fight opponents two or three times their number."
elegant: An expert boxer can defeat multiple untrained wealthy opponents by using skill and tactics -- retreating, picking them off one by one, exploiting their poor conditioning. If this holds in boxing, it holds even more in warfare. Trained warriors can fight far superior numbers of undisciplined rich men.
summary: Socrates uses a boxing analogy: a skilled boxer can defeat multiple wealthy but untrained opponents through superior technique and conditioning. Since the advantage of training over wealth is even greater in warfare than boxing, their warriors can fight enemies two or three times their number.
distilled: Expert training defeats superior numbers of wealthy but undisciplined opponents, in boxing as in warfare.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The boxing analogy reflects the Greek gymnasium culture where military and athletic training overlapped -- The tactical description (retreat, turn, strike) resembles actual Greek light infantry tactics -- This argument parallels Spartan military ideology: a small, well-trained force defeats larger, wealthier armies -- Cross-ref: The Battle of Thermopylae as a historical example Plato's audience would have known -- The 'scorching sun' detail emphasizes the advantage of physical conditioning

narrative:
"I agree with you, for I think you right. And suppose that, before engaging, our citizens send an embassy to one of the two cities, telling them what is the truth: 'Silver and gold we neither have nor are permitted to have, but you may; do you therefore come and help us in war, and take the spoils of the other city.' Who, on hearing these words, would choose to fight against lean wiry dogs, rather than, with the dogs on their side, against fat and tender sheep?"

"That is not likely; and yet there might be a danger to the poor State if the wealth of many States were to be gathered into one."

"But how simple of you to use the term 'State' at all of any but our own!"
refashioned:
"I agree -- you're right. And imagine that before battle, our citizens send ambassadors to one of the two enemy cities with this message: 'We have no silver or gold, and we're forbidden to possess any -- but you may have all you want. Come fight alongside us, and you can keep all the spoils from the other city.' Who would choose to fight against lean, wiry dogs when they could fight alongside those dogs against fat, tender sheep?"

"That seems right. But wouldn't there be a danger if many wealthy cities combined their resources against our poor one?"

"How naive of you to use the word 'city' for any of those places but our own!"
elegant: A city without gold can form alliances by offering wealthy allies the spoils of their enemies. No one would choose to fight lean, hardened warriors when they could fight alongside them against soft, wealthy opponents. As for coalitions of wealthy cities -- they are not true cities at all.
summary: Socrates proposes a diplomatic strategy: offer wealthy allies all war spoils in exchange for their help, since no one would rather fight trained warriors than fight alongside them. When Adeimantus worries about coalitions of wealthy enemies, Socrates challenges whether such places deserve the name 'city' at all.
distilled: A disciplined city without wealth can win allies by offering spoils, and wealthy but divided cities are not true cities at all.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Lean wiry dogs vs. fat tender sheep' is a vivid Platonic image contrasting discipline with self-indulgence -- The diplomatic strategy of offering spoils reflects real Greek alliance practices -- Socrates' correction about the term 'State/city' (polis) sets up the next passage's argument that internally divided cities are not true political communities -- Cross-ref: This anticipates the argument in 4.8 about every city being divided into rich and poor

narrative:
"Why so?"

"You ought to speak of other States in the plural number; not one of them is a city, but many cities, as they say in the game. For indeed any city, however small, is in fact divided into two, one the city of the poor, the other of the rich; these are at war with one another; and in either there are many smaller divisions, and you would be altogether beside the mark if you treated them all as a single State.

But if you deal with them as many, and give the wealth or power or persons of the one to the others, you will always have a great many friends and not many enemies. And your State, while the wise order which has now been prescribed continues to prevail in her, will be the greatest of States, I do not mean to say in reputation or appearance, but in deed and truth, though she number not more than a thousand defenders. A single State which is her equal you will hardly find, either among Hellenes or barbarians, though many that appear to be as great and many times greater."
refashioned:
"Why do you say that?"

"Because you should speak of those other places in the plural. None of them is really one city -- each is many cities, as the saying goes. Every city, no matter how small, is actually divided into at least two: the city of the poor and the city of the rich, and these two are at war with each other. Within each, there are further divisions. You'd be completely wrong to treat any of them as a single unified state.

But if you treat them as the many factions they are, and offer the wealth, power, or people of one faction to another, you'll always have plenty of allies and few enemies. And our city, as long as its wise order holds, will be the greatest of all cities -- not in appearance or reputation, but in reality and truth, even if it has no more than a thousand defenders. You'll hardly find its equal among Greeks or foreigners, though many cities may appear as great or far greater."
elegant: Every conventional city is actually divided into at least two warring cities -- the rich and the poor -- with further divisions within each. Treat these factions as separate entities and you can always find allies among them. A truly unified city, even with only a thousand defenders, will be the greatest in reality if not in appearance.
summary: Socrates argues that every conventional city is really multiple cities at war with each other -- at minimum, the city of the rich versus the city of the poor. By exploiting these divisions, a unified city can always find allies. Their city, though small, will be the greatest in truth because it alone is truly one.
distilled: Every city is really divided into warring factions of rich and poor; only a truly unified city, however small, is genuinely great.
notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Adeimantus -- 'As they say in the game' refers to a board game (petteia) where one piece represents many -- The rich-poor division as civil war is a profound sociological observation that influenced Marx and others -- Cross-ref: Book VIII extensively develops how class conflict drives political degeneration (oligarchy to democracy to tyranny) -- 'Hellenes or barbarians' encompasses the entire known world -- The contrast between appearance and reality is a central Platonic theme -- Cross-ref: Thucydides' account of stasis (civil conflict) in Corcyra

narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And what," I said, "will be the best limit for our rulers to fix when they are considering the size of the State and the amount of territory which they are to include, and beyond which they will not go?"

"What limit would you propose?"

"I would allow the State to increase so far as is consistent with unity; that, I think, is the proper limit."

"Very good," he said.

"Here then," I said, "is another order which will have to be conveyed to our guardians: Let our city be accounted neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficing."

"And surely," said he, "this is not a very severe order which we impose upon them."
refashioned:
"That's absolutely true," he said.

"So what should be the limit our rulers set when deciding the city's size and territory -- beyond which they won't expand?"

"What limit would you suggest?"

"The city should grow only as far as it can while remaining unified. That's the proper limit."

"Good."

"So here's another directive for our guardians: let the city be considered neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficient."

"That doesn't seem like a particularly harsh requirement," he said.
elegant: The city should expand only so far as is consistent with its unity. The proper directive for the guardians: the city should be neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficient.
summary: Socrates proposes that the city's growth should be limited by what preserves its unity. The directive to the guardians is simple: the city should be one and self-sufficient, neither too large nor too small.
distilled: The city should grow only as far as it can remain unified and self-sufficient.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Unity as the limit of growth reflects Greek suspicion of imperial expansion -- 'One and self-sufficing' (autarkeia) is a key concept in Greek political thought, later central to Aristotle's Politics -- Cross-ref: Book II's founding of the city based on mutual need and self-sufficiency -- The idea contrasts with Athenian imperial ambitions that Plato witnessed -- Aristotle (Politics 1326b) later discusses optimal city size in detail

narrative: "And the other," said I, "of which we were speaking before is lighter still -- I mean the duty of degrading the offspring of the guardians when inferior, and of elevating into the rank of guardians the offspring of the lower classes, when naturally superior. The intention was, that, in the case of the citizens generally, each individual should be put to the use for which nature intended him, one to one work, and then every man would do his own business, and be one and not many; and so the whole city would be one and not many."
refashioned: "And the other duty we mentioned earlier is lighter still," I said, "-- the responsibility of demoting the children of guardians when they prove inferior, and promoting the children of lower classes when they show natural superiority. The whole point is that every individual should be assigned the role nature designed them for -- one person, one job. When each person does their own work, each becomes unified in purpose rather than scattered. And so the whole city becomes one rather than many."
elegant: Children of guardians who prove inferior must be demoted; children of lower classes who show natural ability must be elevated. Each person should do the one thing nature fitted them for. When individuals are unified in purpose, the city itself becomes one.
summary: Socrates describes the principle of social mobility based on natural ability: guardian children who prove inferior are demoted while talented children from lower classes are elevated. The goal is that each person does the one work nature intended, making both individual and city unified rather than fragmented.
distilled: Social mobility based on natural ability ensures each person does their proper work, making both individual and city truly unified.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the meritocratic principle of the Republic, remarkable for ancient Greece -- 'One to one work' restates the specialization principle from Book II -- The phrase 'be one and not many' connects individual unity to civic unity, foreshadowing the tripartite soul argument later in Book IV -- Cross-ref: Book III 415a-c, the Allegory of the Metals (gold, silver, bronze souls) -- Cross-ref: The 'Noble Lie' that justifies this social mobility -- This principle is both progressive (mobility) and restrictive (fixed roles)

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "that is not so difficult."

"The regulations which we are prescribing, my good Adeimantus, are not, as might be supposed, a number of great principles, but trifles all, if care be taken, as the saying is, of the one great thing -- a thing, however, which I would rather call, not great, but sufficient for our purpose."

"What may that be?" he asked.

"Education," I said, "and nurture: If our citizens are well educated, and grow into sensible men, they will easily see their way through all these, as well as other matters which I omit; such, for example, as marriage, the possession of women and the procreation of children, which will all follow the general principle that friends have all things in common, as the proverb says."

"That will be the best way of settling them."

"Also," I said, "the State, if once started well, moves with accumulating force like a wheel. For good nurture and education implant good constitutions, and these good constitutions taking root in a good education improve more and more, and this improvement affects the breed in man as in other animals."

"Very possibly," he said.

"Then to sum up: This is the point to which, above all, the attention of our rulers should be directed -- that music and gymnastic be preserved in their original form, and no innovation made. They must do their utmost to maintain them intact. And when any one says that mankind most regard"
refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "that's not too difficult."

"The rules we're laying down, my dear Adeimantus, aren't really a long list of grand principles. They're all minor details -- provided we take care of the one thing that matters. Though I'd call it not so much 'great' as 'sufficient for our purpose.'"

"And what is that?"

"Education and upbringing. If our citizens are properly educated and develop into thoughtful people, they'll figure out all these matters easily enough on their own -- including things I'm leaving aside, like marriage, family arrangements, and child-rearing, all of which will follow the general principle that friends share everything in common."

"That would be the best approach."

"And here's the key insight: once a city gets a good start, it builds momentum like a wheel. Good upbringing and education produce good character, and good character rooted in good education improves further with each generation -- in humans just as in other animals."

"That seems likely."

"So to sum up: the single most important task for our rulers is to preserve music and gymnastic education in their original form, allowing no innovation. They must do their utmost to keep them intact. And when anyone says that people care most about..."
elegant: All the city's regulations are trifles if one great thing is secured: education. Well-educated citizens will solve every problem themselves. A well-started city builds momentum like a wheel -- good education produces good character, which produces better education, improving with each generation. Above all, rulers must preserve the established forms of music and gymnastic education without innovation.
summary: Socrates declares that all regulations are trivial if education is secured. A well-started city gains momentum: good education produces good character, which improves education further across generations. The rulers' supreme task is preserving music and gymnastic education in their original form.
distilled: Education is the one essential foundation; once established well, it creates a virtuous cycle that makes all other regulations unnecessary.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Friends have all things in common' is a Pythagorean proverb Plato frequently invokes -- Cross-ref: Book V will elaborate extensively on the 'possession of women and children' in common -- The wheel metaphor for virtuous cycle is striking -- 'Music and gymnastic' (mousike and gymnastike) encompass all cultural/intellectual and physical education respectively -- Cross-ref: Book III's detailed treatment of musical modes and their effects on character -- The conservative stance on musical innovation reflects a real debate in Athens -- The passage is cut off mid-sentence in the original

narrative:
"The newest song which the singers have,"

they will be afraid that he may be praising, not new songs, but a new kind of song; and this ought not to be praised, or conceived to be the meaning of the poet; for any musical innovation is full of danger to the whole State, and ought to be prohibited. So Damon tells me, and I can quite believe him; -- he says that when modes of music change, the fundamental laws of the State always change with them.
refashioned: When people praise 'the newest song the singers have,' there's a risk they may be praising not just new songs, but a new kind of song entirely -- and that must not be encouraged or assumed to be the poet's meaning. Any innovation in music poses a danger to the entire city and should be prohibited. Damon taught me this, and I believe him completely: when the modes of music change, the fundamental laws of the city always change with them.
elegant: Innovation in music endangers the entire city. As Damon teaches: when musical modes change, the fundamental laws of the state change with them.
summary: Socrates warns against musical innovation, citing Damon's teaching that changes in musical modes inevitably lead to changes in the fundamental laws of the state. New kinds of song -- not merely new songs -- pose a danger to the entire civic order.
distilled: Changes in musical modes inevitably lead to changes in the fundamental laws of the state.
notes: Speakers: Socrates, quoting Homer ('the newest song') and citing Damon -- Damon of Athens was a real music theorist and associate of Pericles, known for the doctrine that musical modes shape character -- The Homer quotation is from the Odyssey (1.351-352) -- The distinction between 'new songs' and 'a new kind of song' is crucial: Plato opposes genre innovation, not new compositions within established forms -- Cross-ref: Book III 398c-400c on the regulation of musical modes -- This conservative cultural theory influenced later political thought about art and social order

narrative:
"Yes," said Adeimantus; "and you may add my suffrage to Damon's and your own."

"Then," I said, "our guardians must lay the foundations of their fortress in music?"

"Yes," he said; "the lawlessness of which you speak too easily steals in."

"Yes," I replied, "in the form of amusement; and at first sight it appears harmless."

"Why, yes," he said, "and there is no harm; were it not that little by little this spirit of licence, finding a home, imperceptibly penetrates into manners and customs; whence, issuing with greater force, it invades contracts between man and man, and from contracts goes on to laws and constitutions, in utter recklessness, ending at last, Socrates, by an overthrow of all rights, private as well as public."

"Is that true?" I said.

"That is my belief," he replied.

"Then, as I was saying, our youth should be trained from the first in a stricter system, for if amusements become lawless, and the youths themselves become lawless, they can never grow up into well-conducted and virtuous citizens."

"Very true," he said.

"And when they have made a good beginning in play, and by the help of music have gained the habit of good order, then this habit of order, in a manner how unlike the lawless play of the others! will accompany them in all their actions and be a principle of growth to them, and if there be any fallen places in the State will raise them up again."

"Very true," he said.

"Thus educated, they will invent for themselves any lesser rules which their predecessors have altogether neglected."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean such things as these: -- when the young are to be silent before their elders; how they are to show respect to them by standing and making them sit; what honour is due to parents; what garments or shoes are to be worn; the mode of dressing the hair; deportment and manners in general. You would agree with me?"

"Yes."

"But there is, I think, small wisdom in legislating about such matters -- I doubt if it is ever done; nor are any precise written enactments about them likely to be lasting."
refashioned:
"Yes," said Adeimantus, "add my vote to Damon's and yours."

"Then our guardians must build their fortress on the foundation of music?"

"Yes -- the lawlessness you describe creeps in all too easily."

"It does, disguised as harmless amusement."

"Exactly. It seems harmless at first. But little by little, this spirit of license finds a foothold and imperceptibly seeps into manners and customs. From there, with growing force, it invades business dealings and contracts. From contracts it moves on to laws and constitutions, with complete recklessness, until finally, Socrates, it overthrows all rights, both private and public."

"Is that really how it works?"

"That is what I believe."

"Then our youth must be trained from the start in a stricter system. If their amusements become lawless, they themselves become lawless, and they can never grow into well-conducted, virtuous citizens."

"True."

"But when they make a good beginning in play and through music develop habits of order, those habits -- so different from lawless play -- will accompany them in everything they do, becoming a principle of growth. If any part of the city has fallen into disrepair, those habits will restore it."

"True."

"Citizens educated this way will figure out for themselves all the minor rules their predecessors neglected."

"Such as?"

"Things like: when the young should be silent before their elders, how to show respect by standing and offering seats, what honor is owed to parents, appropriate clothing and shoes, hairstyles, deportment and manners generally."

"Yes, I'd agree."

"But there's little wisdom in trying to legislate such things. I doubt it's ever been done successfully, and precise written rules about them never last."
elegant: Lawlessness enters the city disguised as harmless amusement. It begins in play, seeps into manners and customs, invades contracts and laws, and ultimately overthrows all rights. The antidote is strict early education in music: youth who develop habits of order through disciplined play carry those habits into every sphere of life. Well-educated citizens will discover proper manners for themselves -- such things cannot be legislated.
summary: Lawlessness enters disguised as harmless amusement in music and play, gradually corrupting manners, contracts, laws, and eventually all rights. The solution is strict early education: youth trained in orderly music develop habits of order that pervade their entire lives. Well-educated citizens will discover proper social manners on their own -- legislating such details is futile.
distilled: Lawlessness creeps in through amusement and gradually corrupts everything; only disciplined early education in music can instill the habits of order that prevent this decay.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus in extended dialogue -- Adeimantus unusually delivers the key argument about lawlessness spreading from amusement to constitutions -- This 'slippery slope' from cultural to political corruption is one of Plato's most influential ideas -- Cross-ref: Book III on education in music and gymnastic -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the progression of political degeneration -- The list of manners (silence before elders, standing, hairstyles) reflects actual Greek social norms -- The claim that manners cannot be legislated is notable given the Republic's extensive legislation elsewhere -- 'Fallen places in the State' suggests music education has restorative, not just preventive, power

narrative:
"Impossible."

"It would seem, Adeimantus, that the direction in which education starts a man, will determine his future life. Does not like always attract like?"

"To be sure."

"Until some one rare and grand result is reached which may be good, and may be the reverse of good?"

"That is not to be denied."

"And for this reason," I said, "I shall not attempt to legislate further about them."
refashioned:
"Impossible to legislate."

"It seems, Adeimantus, that the direction education gives a person at the start determines the course of their entire life. Like attracts like, doesn't it?"

"Of course."

"Until some rare and significant outcome is reached -- which may be good, or may be the opposite?"

"That can't be denied."

"And for this reason, I won't attempt to legislate further on such matters."
elegant: The direction education gives a person at the start determines their entire future. Like attracts like, building momentum toward some grand result -- for good or ill. Therefore, further legislation on minor matters is unnecessary.
summary: Socrates concludes that education's initial direction determines the entire course of a person's life, as like attracts like, building toward some significant result -- good or bad. This is why further legislation on manners and customs is unnecessary.
distilled: The initial direction of education determines a person's entire life trajectory, making detailed legislation on conduct unnecessary.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Like attracts like' echoes Homer and is a common Greek proverb -- The idea of education as determining trajectory connects to the wheel metaphor in 4.11 -- The acknowledgment that the grand result 'may be the reverse of good' shows Plato's awareness that education can compound in negative directions too -- Cross-ref: The allegory of the cave (Book VII) as the ultimate statement about education's transformative power -- This passage justifies the Republic's focus on education over legislation

narrative:
"Naturally enough," he replied.

"Well, and about the business of the agora, and the ordinary dealings between man and man, or again about agreements with artisans; about insult and injury, or the commencement of actions, and the appointment of juries, what would you say? There may also arise questions about any impositions and extractions of market and harbour dues which may be required, and in general about the regulations of markets, police, harbours, and the like. But, oh heavens! shall we condescend to legislate on any of these particulars?"
refashioned:
"Naturally," he replied.

"And what about marketplace business, everyday dealings between people, contracts with tradesmen, matters of insult and injury, filing lawsuits, appointing juries? There are also questions about market taxes, harbor fees, and regulations for markets, police, and harbors generally. But good heavens -- should we really bother legislating about any of these details?"
elegant: What about marketplace regulations, contracts, lawsuits, taxes, harbor fees, and policing? Should we legislate on any of these details? Surely not.
summary: Socrates lists the mundane details of civic life -- marketplace dealings, contracts, lawsuits, juries, taxes, harbor regulations -- and asks rhetorically whether they should bother legislating about any of them.
distilled: Mundane civic regulations about markets, courts, and commerce should not need explicit legislation in a well-educated city.
notes: Speakers: Socrates poses the rhetorical question to Adeimantus -- The agora (marketplace) was the commercial and social center of Greek cities -- The list covers commercial law, tort law, procedural law, and regulatory law -- Plato's dismissal of detailed legislation reflects his belief that education, not rules, produces good behavior -- Cross-ref: This contrasts with the Laws, Plato's later dialogue, which legislates precisely these kinds of details -- The exclamation 'oh heavens!' conveys Socrates' exasperation at the thought of micromanaging civic life

narrative:
"I think," he said, "that there is no need to impose laws about them on good men; what regulations are necessary they will find out soon enough for themselves."

"Yes," I said, "my friend, if God will only preserve to them the laws which we have given them."

"And without divine help," said Adeimantus, "they will go on for ever making and mending their laws and their lives in the hope of attaining perfection."

"You would compare them," I said, "to those invalids who, having no self-restraint, will not leave off their habits of intemperance?"

"Exactly."

"Yes," I said; "and what a delightful life they lead! they are always doctoring and increasing and complicating their disorders, and always fancying that they will be cured by any nostrum which anybody advises them to try."

"Such cases are very common," he said, "with invalids of this sort."

"Yes," I replied; "and the charming thing is that they deem him their worst enemy who tells them the truth, which is simply that, unless they give up eating and drinking and wenching and idling, neither drug nor cautery nor spell nor amulet nor any other remedy will avail."

"Charming!" he replied. "I see nothing charming in going into a passion with a man who tells you what is right."

"These gentlemen," I said, "do not seem to be in your good graces."
refashioned:
"I don't think," he said, "there's any need to impose such laws on good people. They'll figure out whatever regulations are necessary on their own."

"Yes, my friend -- provided God preserves the fundamental laws we've given them."

"And without divine help," said Adeimantus, "they'll go on forever tinkering with their laws and their lives, always hoping to reach perfection."

"You'd compare them to those chronic invalids who lack self-restraint and refuse to give up their unhealthy habits?"

"Exactly."

"What a delightful life they lead! Always doctoring themselves, making their conditions more complicated, and forever believing the next remedy someone recommends will finally cure them."

"That's extremely common with such people."

"And here's the charming part: they consider their worst enemy the person who tells them the truth -- that unless they stop overeating, overdrinking, and overindulging, no drug, surgery, spell, or charm will do any good."

"Charming indeed! I see nothing charming about getting angry at someone who tells you what's right."

"These people don't seem to be in your good graces," I said.
elegant: Good people need no detailed laws -- they'll figure things out themselves. But without sound foundations, people endlessly tinker with laws hoping for perfection, like invalids who refuse to change their habits yet constantly try new remedies. The ultimate irony: they despise whoever tells them the truth -- that no cure will work until they change their way of life.
summary: Good people don't need detailed laws. But without sound foundations, citizens endlessly tinker with legislation hoping for perfection, like invalids who refuse to change their habits yet keep trying new cures. Most tellingly, they hate whoever tells them the truth: that no remedy works without changing one's way of life.
distilled: Endlessly reforming laws without changing fundamental habits is like an intemperate invalid trying every remedy while refusing to change the lifestyle causing the disease.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The invalid analogy is one of Plato's favorites; medicine-as-metaphor for politics runs throughout the Republic -- Cross-ref: Book III 405a-410a on medicine and the healthy city -- 'Eating and drinking and wenching and idling' covers the four main forms of intemperance -- The idea that people hate truth-tellers anticipates the philosopher's reception in the cave allegory (Book VII) -- Cross-ref: Socrates' own fate as a truth-teller Athens rejected -- 'Cautery, spell, amulet' juxtaposes rational and irrational medicine, as Plato often does

narrative:
"Assuredly not."

"Nor would you praise the behaviour of States which act like the men whom I was just now describing. For are there not ill-ordered States in which the citizens are forbidden under pain of death to alter the constitution; and yet he who most sweetly courts those who live under this regime and indulges them and fawns upon them and is skilful in anticipating and gratifying their humours is held to be a great and good statesman -- do not these States resemble the persons whom I was describing?"
refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Then you wouldn't praise states that behave the same way. Aren't there badly governed states where citizens are forbidden on pain of death from altering the constitution -- yet the person who most skillfully flatters those in power, indulges them, fawns over them, and anticipates their every whim is celebrated as a great statesman? Don't these states resemble the invalids I was just describing?"
elegant: Badly governed states forbid constitutional change on pain of death, yet celebrate as great statesmen those who most skillfully flatter and indulge the powerful. Such states are like invalids who refuse to change their habits while trying every superficial remedy.
summary: Socrates extends the invalid analogy to states: badly governed cities forbid constitutional change yet celebrate as great statesmen those who most skillfully flatter and indulge the ruling class. Such states, like chronic invalids, refuse fundamental change while rewarding those who enable their dysfunction.
distilled: States that forbid constitutional reform while celebrating skilled flatterers as great statesmen are like invalids who refuse to change their harmful habits.
notes: Speakers: Socrates poses the analogy to Adeimantus -- The description of flattering statesmen recalls the Gorgias, where Socrates distinguishes genuine statesmanship from flattery (kolakeia) -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 463a-466a on rhetoric as flattery versus true political art -- The 'pain of death' for constitutional change may reference actual Greek laws (e.g., Spartan prohibition on changing Lycurgan laws) -- This passage anticipates Book VIII's critique of democratic and tyrannical politicians -- The word 'sweetly' (hedeos) connects political flattery to the pleasure-driven life Plato opposes

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "the States are as bad as the men; and I am very far from praising them."

"But do you not admire," I said, "the coolness and dexterity of these ready ministers of political corruption?"

"Yes," he said, "I do; but not of all of them, for there are some whom the applause of the multitude has deluded into the belief that they are really statesmen, and these are not much to be admired."

"What do you mean?" I said; "you should have more feeling for them. When a man cannot measure, and a great many others who cannot measure declare that he is four cubits high, can he help believing what they say?"

"Nay," he said, "certainly not in that case."

"Well, then, do not be angry with them; for are they not as good as a play, trying their hand at paltry reforms such as I was describing; they are always fancying that by legislation they will make an end of frauds in contracts, and the other rascalities which I was mentioning, not knowing that they are in reality cutting off the heads of a hydra?"

"Yes," he said; "that is just what they are doing."

"I conceive," I said, "that the true legislator will not trouble himself with this class of enactments whether concerning laws or the constitution either in an ill-ordered or in a well-ordered State; for in the former they are quite useless, and in the latter there will be no difficulty in devising them; and many of them will naturally flow out of our previous regulations."

"What, then," he said, "is still remaining to us of the work of legislation?"

"Nothing to us," I replied; "but to Apollo, the God of Delphi, there remains the ordering of the greatest and noblest and chiefest things of all."
refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "those states are as bad as those invalids, and I'm far from praising them."

"But don't you admire the coolness and skill of these willing agents of political corruption?"

"I do -- though not all of them. Some have been deluded by popular applause into genuinely believing they're real statesmen. Those aren't much to admire."

"You should feel sorry for them! When a man can't measure, and a crowd of equally ignorant people tell him he's four cubits tall, how can he help believing them?"

"He can't, I suppose."

"So don't be angry with them. They're really quite entertaining -- trying their hand at petty reforms, always imagining they can legislate away fraud and corruption, not realizing they're cutting off the heads of a hydra."

"That's exactly what they're doing."

"The true legislator won't bother with this kind of legislation, whether in a well-ordered or poorly ordered state. In a bad state, such laws are useless. In a good state, they're unnecessary -- most of them will flow naturally from the foundational laws we've already established."

"Then what legislative work remains for us?"

"Nothing for us. But for Apollo, the god of Delphi, the greatest, noblest, and most important matters of all remain."
elegant: Politicians deluded by popular applause into thinking they're statesmen try endlessly to legislate away corruption -- cutting off the heads of a hydra. The true legislator avoids such pettiness: in a bad state, detailed laws are useless; in a good state, they flow naturally from sound foundations. Only the greatest matters -- religion and the sacred -- remain, and these belong to Apollo at Delphi.
summary: Politicians deluded by popular applause attempt endless piecemeal reforms, not realizing they're fighting a hydra. The true legislator ignores such details: in bad states they're useless, in good states they're unnecessary. The only remaining legislative task -- ordering religion and sacred rites -- belongs not to human legislators but to Apollo at Delphi.
distilled: Piecemeal legal reforms are like cutting off a hydra's heads; the true legislator relies on foundational education and defers sacred matters to the divine authority of Delphi.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The hydra reference is to the Lernaean Hydra slain by Heracles, whose heads regrew when cut -- 'Four cubits high' (about six feet) -- the joke is that collective ignorance reinforces individual delusion -- Apollo at Delphi (the Oracle) was the supreme religious authority in Greece -- Cross-ref: The Apology, where Socrates' philosophical mission begins with the Delphic oracle -- Deferring to Delphi on religious matters shows Plato's caution about legislating religion -- Cross-ref: Laws, Book V, where Plato similarly defers religious legislation to Delphi -- The distinction between true and apparent statesmen parallels the true vs. apparent guardians of 4.4

narrative:
"Which are they?" he said.

"The institution of temples and sacrifices, and the entire service of gods, demigods, and heroes; also the ordering of the repositories of the dead, and the rites which have to be observed by him who would propitiate the inhabitants of the world below. These are matters of which we are ignorant ourselves, and as founders of a city we should be unwise in trusting them to any interpreter but our ancestral deity. He is the god who sits in the centre, on the navel of the earth, and he is the interpreter of religion to all mankind."
refashioned:
"What matters are those?" he asked.

"The establishment of temples and sacrifices, and the entire worship of gods, demigods, and heroes. Also the proper treatment of the dead, and the rites required to honor those in the underworld. These are matters we know nothing about ourselves, and as founders of a city, we'd be foolish to entrust them to any authority other than our ancestral god. Apollo sits at the center, on the navel of the earth, and serves as the interpreter of religion for all humanity."
elegant: The greatest matters of legislation -- temples, sacrifices, worship of gods and heroes, funeral rites, and the honoring of the dead -- belong to Apollo at Delphi. As founders, we are ignorant of such things and must defer to the god who sits at the earth's navel, the interpreter of religion for all mankind.
summary: The greatest legislative matters -- establishing temples, sacrifices, divine worship, funerary rites, and honoring the dead -- must be left to Apollo at Delphi, the ancestral god who sits at the earth's navel and interprets religion for all humanity. Human founders are too ignorant to legislate on sacred matters.
distilled: Sacred matters -- temples, sacrifices, worship, and rites for the dead -- must be left to Apollo at Delphi, the divine interpreter of religion.
notes: Speakers: Socrates responds to Adeimantus -- The 'navel of the earth' (omphalos) was a sacred stone at Delphi believed to mark the earth's center -- Apollo was considered the 'ancestral deity' (patrios theos) of the Ionians, including Athenians -- 'Demigods and heroes' (daimones and heroi) were important categories in Greek religion -- 'Inhabitants of the world below' refers to the dead in Hades -- Cross-ref: Book III 386a-387c on proper representation of the afterlife -- Cross-ref: Phaedo on the soul's fate after death -- This passage shows Plato's conservative stance on traditional religion, even within his revolutionary political theory

narrative:
"You are right, and we will do as you propose."

"But where, amid all this, is justice? Son of Ariston, tell me where. Now that our city has been made habitable, light a candle and search, and get your brother and Polemarchus and the rest of our friends to help, and let us see where in it we can discover justice and where injustice, and in what they differ from one another, and which of them the man who would be happy should have for his portion, whether seen or unseen by gods and men."
refashioned:
"You're right, and we'll follow your proposal."

"But where in all of this is justice? Son of Ariston, tell me! Now that our city has been made livable, light a torch and search. Get your brother and Polemarchus and the rest of our friends to help. Let's see where in this city we can find justice and where injustice lies, how they differ from each other, and which of them the person who wants to be happy should choose -- whether or not gods and men are watching."
elegant: Now that the city is built, the hunt for justice begins. Socrates calls on Glaucon (son of Ariston), his brother, Polemarchus, and the others to search the city and find where justice and injustice reside, how they differ, and which leads to happiness -- whether observed by gods and men or not.
summary: With the city now constructed, Socrates pivots to the central question: where is justice? He calls on Glaucon (son of Ariston), Adeimantus, Polemarchus, and the others to search the city for justice and injustice, to understand how they differ, and to determine which leads to happiness regardless of whether gods and men observe it.
distilled: With the city built, Socrates turns to the central quest: finding where justice and injustice reside and which leads to true happiness.
notes: Speakers: Adeimantus agrees, then Socrates addresses Glaucon directly as 'Son of Ariston' -- Ariston was the father of both Glaucon and Adeimantus (and Plato himself) -- Polemarchus was the host of the gathering in Book I and gave the initial definition of justice -- 'Whether seen or unseen by gods and men' directly references Glaucon's challenge in Book II (360b-362c), where he asked whether justice is good in itself or only when observed -- The candle/torch metaphor for philosophical inquiry is characteristic of Plato -- This passage marks the major transition from city-building to the analysis of the four virtues -- Cross-ref: Book I's initial definitions of justice by Cephalus, Polemarchus, and Thrasymachus

narrative:
"Nonsense," said Glaucon: "did you not promise to search yourself, saying that for you not to help justice in her need would be an impiety?"

"I do not deny that I said so, and as you remind me, I will be as good as my word; but you must join."

"We will," he replied.

"Well, then, I hope to make the discovery in this way: I mean to begin with the assumption that our State, if rightly ordered, is perfect."

"That is most certain."

"And being perfect, is therefore wise and valiant and temperate and just."

"That is likewise clear."

"And whichever of these qualities we find in the State, the one which is not found will be the residue?"

"Very good."

"If there were four things, and we were searching for one of them, wherever it might be, the one sought for might be known to us from the first, and there would be no further trouble; or we might know the other three first, and then the fourth would clearly be the one left."

"Very true," he said.

"And is not a similar method to be pursued about the virtues, which are also four in number?"

"Clearly."

"First among the virtues found in the State, wisdom comes into view, and in this I detect a certain peculiarity."

"What is that?"

"The State which we have been describing is said to be wise as being good in counsel?"

"Very true."

"And good counsel is clearly a kind of knowledge, for not by ignorance, but by knowledge, do men counsel well?"

"Clearly."

"And the kinds of knowledge in a State are many and diverse?"

"Of course."

"There is the knowledge of the carpenter; but is that the sort of knowledge which gives a city the title of wise and good in counsel?"

"Certainly not; that would only give a city the reputation of skill in carpentering."

"Then a city is not to be called wise because possessing a knowledge which counsels for the best about wooden implements?"

"Certainly not."

"Nor by reason of a knowledge which advises about brazen pots," I said, "nor as possessing any other similar knowledge?"
refashioned:
"Nonsense," said Glaucon. "Didn't you promise to search for justice yourself? You said it would be impious not to help justice in her hour of need."

"I don't deny saying that, and since you remind me, I'll keep my word. But you must join the search."

"We will."

"Good. Here's my approach: I'll begin with the assumption that our city, if rightly ordered, is perfect."

"That's certain."

"And being perfect, it must be wise, courageous, temperate, and just."

"Clearly."

"So whichever of these four virtues we identify, the ones remaining will be whatever we haven't found yet."

"Agreed."

"If we're searching for one thing among four, we might recognize it immediately. Or we might identify the other three first, and the fourth would be whatever is left."

"True."

"We can use the same method with the virtues, which are also four."

"Clearly."

"Wisdom comes into view first, and I notice something peculiar about it."

"What's that?"

"Our city is called wise because it possesses good counsel, correct?"

"Yes."

"And good counsel is a form of knowledge -- people counsel well through knowledge, not ignorance."

"Obviously."

"But there are many kinds of knowledge in a city."

"Of course."

"Take carpentry -- does that kind of knowledge make a city wise and good in counsel?"

"No, it would only make the city skilled in carpentry."

"So a city isn't called wise because it has knowledge about the best use of wooden implements?"

"Certainly not."

"Or knowledge about bronze pots, or any other specialized craft?"
elegant: The method: our perfect city must be wise, courageous, temperate, and just. Find three virtues, and the fourth is whatever remains. Wisdom comes first. The city is wise through good counsel, which requires knowledge -- but not the knowledge of any particular craft like carpentry or metalwork. Those make a city skilled, not wise.
summary: Socrates proposes a method of elimination: the perfect city must possess four virtues -- wisdom, courage, temperance, and justice. Find three, and the fourth is the remainder. Beginning with wisdom, he argues that the city's wisdom lies not in any specialized craft knowledge (carpentry, metalwork) but in something else entirely.
distilled: The city's wisdom is not the specialized knowledge of any craft; it must be a higher form of knowledge -- good counsel about the city as a whole.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon take over the dialogue from Adeimantus -- The four cardinal virtues (wisdom, courage, temperance, justice) are a Platonic formulation that became foundational in Western ethics -- The method of elimination (finding justice as the residue after identifying the other three) is a distinctive logical strategy -- The distinction between craft-knowledge (techne) and political wisdom (sophia) is central to Plato's epistemology -- Cross-ref: Book I where Thrasymachus compares justice to a craft -- Cross-ref: The Protagoras on whether virtue is one or many -- 'Good counsel' (euboulia) is later important in Aristotle's Ethics

narrative:
"Not by reason of any of them," he said.

"Nor yet by reason of a knowledge which cultivates the earth; that would give the city the name of agricultural?"

"Yes."

"Well," I said, "and is there any knowledge in our recently founded State among any of the citizens which advises, not about any particular thing in the State, but about the whole, and considers how a State can best deal with itself and with other States?"

"There certainly is."

"And what is this knowledge, and among whom is it found?" I asked.

"It is the knowledge of the guardians," he replied, "and is found among those whom we were just now describing as perfect guardians."

"And what is the name which the city derives from the possession of this sort of knowledge?"

"The name of good in counsel and truly wise."

"And will there be in our city more of these true guardians or more smiths?"

"The smiths," he replied, "will be far more numerous."

"Will not the guardians be the smallest of all the classes who receive a name from the profession of some kind of knowledge?"

"Much the smallest."

"And so by reason of the smallest part or class, and of the knowledge which resides in this presiding and ruling part of itself, the whole State, being thus constituted according to nature, will be wise; and this, which has the only knowledge worthy to be called wisdom, has been ordained by nature to be of all classes the least."

"Most true."

"Thus, then," I said, "the nature and place in the State of one of the four virtues has somehow or other been discovered."

"And, in my humble opinion, very satisfactorily discovered," he replied.

"Again," I said, "there is no difficulty in seeing the nature of courage, and in what part that quality resides which gives the name of courageous to the State."

"How do you mean?"

"Why," I said, "every one who calls any State courageous or cowardly, will be thinking of the part which fights and goes out to war on the State's behalf."

"No one," he replied, "would ever think of any other."

"Certainly not. The rest of the citizens may be courageous or may be cowardly, but their courage or cowardice will not, as I conceive, have the effect of making the city either the one or the other."
refashioned:
"No, none of those," he said.

"Nor knowledge of farming -- that would only make the city agricultural."

"Right."

"Then is there any knowledge in our newly founded city that advises not about any particular matter, but about the city as a whole -- how it can best manage its internal affairs and its relations with other cities?"

"There certainly is."

"What is this knowledge, and who possesses it?"

"It's the knowledge of the guardians -- those we described as the perfect guardians."

"And what does this knowledge make the city?"

"Good in counsel and truly wise."

"Are there more of these true guardians in our city, or more smiths?"

"Far more smiths."

"Are the guardians the smallest class defined by any kind of knowledge?"

"By far the smallest."

"So the entire city derives its wisdom from its smallest class -- the knowledge that resides in the presiding and ruling part. This is the only knowledge worthy of the name 'wisdom,' and nature has ordained that the class possessing it be the smallest of all."

"Exactly right."

"So we've discovered the nature and location of the first of our four virtues."

"And quite satisfactorily, in my opinion."

"Next, courage. It's not hard to see where this virtue resides."

"How so?"

"When anyone calls a city courageous or cowardly, they're thinking of the part that fights and goes to war on the city's behalf."

"No one would think of any other part."

"The rest of the citizens may be personally brave or cowardly, but that won't determine whether the city itself is called courageous."
elegant: The city's wisdom resides not in any specialized craft but in the knowledge of the guardians -- knowledge of the city as a whole, how it should manage itself and its relations. This ruling knowledge, found in the smallest class, makes the whole city wise. Similarly, the city's courage belongs to the warrior class -- the part that fights on its behalf. Individual citizens' bravery doesn't determine the city's character.
summary: The city's wisdom comes from the guardians' unique knowledge -- not about any craft, but about the city as a whole and its dealings with other cities. Though the guardians are the smallest class, their knowledge makes the entire city wise. Moving to courage, this virtue resides in the warrior class: the city is called courageous based on its soldiers, not the personal bravery of other citizens.
distilled: Wisdom belongs to the smallest ruling class who know the city as a whole; courage belongs to the warrior class who fight on its behalf.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between knowledge of parts and knowledge of the whole is fundamental to Platonic philosophy -- The smallest class possessing the highest knowledge mirrors the soul's structure (reason is smallest but should rule) -- Cross-ref: The philosopher-king argument in Books V-VII develops this idea that the wisest, not the most numerous, should rule -- The transition from wisdom to courage follows the hierarchical structure: guardians (wisdom), auxiliaries (courage) -- Cross-ref: This maps onto the tripartite soul: reason (wisdom), spirit (courage), appetite (temperance) -- The civic courage of the warrior class anticipates the distinction from individual courage in 4.23-4.26

narrative:
"The city will be courageous in virtue of a portion of herself which preserves under all circumstances that opinion about the nature of things to be feared and not to be feared in which our legislator educated them; and this is what you term courage."

"I should like to hear what you are saying once more, for I do not think that I perfectly understand you."

"I mean that courage is a kind of salvation."

"Salvation of what?"

"Of the opinion respecting things to be feared, what they are and of what nature, which the law implants through education; and I mean by the words 'under all circumstances' to intimate that in pleasure or in pain, or under the influence of desire or fear, a man preserves, and does not lose this opinion. Shall I give you an illustration?"
refashioned:
"The city is courageous because one part of it preserves, under all circumstances, the correct opinion about what should and shouldn't be feared -- the opinion instilled by the lawgiver through education. That is what I mean by courage."

"I'd like to hear that again. I don't think I fully understand."

"Courage is a kind of preservation."

"Preservation of what?"

"Of the opinion about what is truly to be feared and what is not -- the opinion that law implants through education. When I say 'under all circumstances,' I mean that a person holds onto this opinion whether experiencing pleasure or pain, whether gripped by desire or fear. Would an illustration help?"
elegant: Courage is the preservation of the law-educated opinion about what truly should and should not be feared -- maintained under all circumstances, whether in pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
summary: Socrates defines civic courage as the preservation of correct opinion about what should and should not be feared -- an opinion implanted by law through education -- maintained under all circumstances, whether in pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
distilled: Courage is the steadfast preservation of educated opinion about what is truly to be feared, regardless of pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
notes: Speakers: Socrates defines courage, Glaucon asks for clarification -- The definition of courage as 'preservation of opinion' (soteria tes doxes) is distinctive to the Republic -- This is civic, not philosophical, courage -- it's right opinion, not knowledge -- Cross-ref: The Laches, Plato's dialogue dedicated entirely to defining courage -- Cross-ref: Protagoras 349d-360e where courage is discussed as knowledge of what is and isn't to be feared -- The four threats to courage (pleasure, pain, desire, fear) correspond to the four solvents in the dye analogy that follows -- Glaucon's request for clarification is genuine, not Socratic irony

narrative:
"If you please."

"You know," I said, "that dyers, when they want to dye wool for making the true sea-purple, begin by selecting their white colour first; this they prepare and dress with much care and pains, in order that the white ground may take the purple hue in full perfection. The dyeing then proceeds; and whatever is dyed in this manner becomes a fast colour, and no washing either with lyes or without them can take away the bloom. But, when the ground has not been duly prepared, you will have noticed how poor is the look either of purple or of any other colour."

"Yes," he said; "I know that they have a washed-out and ridiculous appearance."
refashioned:
"Please do."

"You know how dyers work when they want to produce true sea-purple wool. First, they carefully select white wool. Then they prepare and treat it with great care so the white ground will absorb the purple dye perfectly. Once dyed this way, the color becomes permanent -- no amount of washing, with lye or without, can fade the bloom. But when the wool hasn't been properly prepared, the result, whether purple or any other color, looks washed-out and ridiculous."

"Yes, I've noticed that."
elegant: To produce true sea-purple, dyers first carefully prepare white wool so it absorbs the dye perfectly, producing a color that no washing can fade. Without proper preparation, the result is washed-out and ridiculous.
summary: Socrates uses the analogy of dyeing wool: to achieve lasting sea-purple, dyers must first carefully prepare white wool so it absorbs the dye permanently. Without proper preparation, the color washes out. This parallels how education must prepare the soul to hold courage fast.
distilled: Just as proper preparation of wool makes dye permanent, proper education makes courageous opinion permanent in the soul.
notes: Speakers: Socrates illustrates, Glaucon confirms -- Sea-purple (Tyrian purple) was the most expensive dye in the ancient world, produced from murex sea snails -- The analogy maps precisely: white wool = the right natural character, careful preparation = education in music and gymnastic, the dye = correct opinion about what to fear, washing/lyes = pleasure, pain, desire, and fear -- Cross-ref: Book III's emphasis on selecting the right natures for guardian education -- The 'washed-out and ridiculous appearance' describes what happens when untrained people try to be courageous -- This is one of Plato's most vivid craft analogies

narrative: "Then now," I said, "you will understand what our object was in selecting our soldiers, and educating them in music and gymnastic; we were contriving influences which would prepare them to take the dye of the laws in perfection, and the colour of their opinion about dangers and of every other opinion was to be indelibly fixed by their nurture and training, not to be washed away by such potent lyes as pleasure -- mightier agent far in washing the soul than any soda or lye; or by sorrow, fear, and desire, the mightiest of all other solvents. And this sort of universal saving power of true opinion in conformity with law about real and false dangers I call and maintain to be courage, unless you disagree."
refashioned: "Now you can understand our purpose in selecting soldiers and educating them in music and gymnastic. We were creating the conditions for them to absorb the dye of the laws perfectly. Their opinions about danger -- and every other important matter -- were to be indelibly fixed by their upbringing and training, impossible to wash away. Not by pleasure, which is a far more powerful solvent of the soul than any lye. Not by sorrow, fear, or desire -- the mightiest solvents of all.
