This universal power to preserve true, law-conforming opinion about real and false dangers is what I call courage -- unless you disagree."
elegant: Education in music and gymnastic prepares the soul to absorb the dye of law so deeply that no solvent can wash it away -- not pleasure (the most potent), nor sorrow, fear, or desire. Courage is the power to preserve true opinion about what is and isn't to be feared, in conformity with law.
summary: Socrates completes the dye analogy: education in music and gymnastic prepares soldiers to absorb law's teachings so deeply they become permanent. Neither pleasure (the most powerful solvent of the soul), nor sorrow, fear, nor desire can wash away their trained opinions about danger. This preservation of law-educated opinion about real and false dangers is courage.
distilled: Courage is the power to preserve law-educated opinion about danger against all solvents -- pleasure, sorrow, fear, and desire.
notes: Speakers: Socrates delivers the formal definition of courage -- Pleasure is identified as the most potent threat to courage, more dangerous than fear itself -- a counterintuitive insight -- The four solvents (pleasure, sorrow, fear, desire) map onto the four threats mentioned in 4.23 -- 'Universal saving power' (soteria dia pantos) emphasizes that courage must hold under all conditions -- This definition is explicitly civic courage (conformity with law), not philosophical courage -- Cross-ref: Laches 194e-199e where Nicias defines courage as knowledge of what is to be feared -- Cross-ref: Book III 412e-413c on testing guardians with pleasures and pains

narrative:
"But I agree," he replied; "for I suppose that you mean to exclude mere uninstructed courage, such as that of a wild beast or of a slave -- this, in your opinion, is not the courage which the law ordains, and ought to have another name."

"Most certainly."

"Then I may infer courage to be such as you describe?"

"Why, yes," said I, "you may, and if you add the words 'of a citizen,' you will not be far wrong; -- hereafter, if you like, we will carry the examination further, but at present we are seeking not for courage but justice; and for the purpose of our enquiry we have said enough."

"You are right," he replied.

"Two virtues remain to be discovered in the State -- first temperance, and then justice which is the end of our search."

"Very true."

"Now, can we find justice without troubling ourselves about temperance?"

"I do not know how that can be accomplished," he said, "nor do I desire that justice should be brought to light and temperance lost sight of; and therefore I wish that you would do me the favour of considering temperance first."

"Certainly," I replied, "I should not be justified in refusing your request."

"Then consider," he said.

"Yes," I replied; "I will; and as far as I can at present see, the virtue of temperance has more of the nature of harmony and symphony than the preceding."

"How so?" he asked.

"Temperance," I replied, "is the ordering or controlling of certain pleasures and desires; this is curiously enough implied in the saying of 'a man being his own master'; and other traces of the same notion may be found in language."

"No doubt," he said.

"There is something ridiculous in the expression 'master of himself'; for the master is also the servant and the servant the master; and in all these modes of speaking the same person is denoted."
refashioned:
"I agree," he replied, "because I assume you're excluding mere untrained courage -- the kind shown by wild animals or slaves. That isn't the courage ordained by law and deserves a different name."

"Exactly."

"So courage is what you've described?"

"Yes -- and if you add 'of a citizen,' you won't be far off. We could examine this further later, but right now we're searching for justice, and we've said enough about courage."

"Fair enough."

"Two virtues remain: first temperance, then justice, which is our ultimate goal."

"True."

"Can we find justice without examining temperance?"

"I don't see how," he said, "and I wouldn't want justice to come to light while temperance is ignored. Please consider temperance first."

"I'd be wrong to refuse. Let me think... As far as I can see, temperance has more the nature of harmony and symphony than the virtues we've examined so far."

"How so?"

"Temperance is the ordering or controlling of certain pleasures and desires. This is curiously implied in the expression 'being master of oneself.' You can find similar hints in language."

"No doubt."

"But there's something absurd about 'master of oneself' -- the master is also the servant, and the servant is the master. The same person is both."
elegant: Mere animal fearlessness or slave endurance is not true courage -- courage is specifically a citizen's educated preservation of right opinion. Moving to temperance: it resembles harmony more than the other virtues. It is the ordering of pleasures and desires, implied in the paradoxical expression 'master of oneself' -- where the same person is both master and servant.
summary: Glaucon notes that uninstructed courage (of beasts or slaves) is excluded from this definition -- true courage is a citizen's virtue. Two virtues remain: temperance and justice. Temperance, unlike the previous virtues, has the nature of harmony. It means ordering pleasures and desires, as hinted by the paradoxical expression 'master of oneself,' where the same person is both ruler and ruled.
distilled: True courage is educated civic virtue, not animal fearlessness; temperance is the harmonious ordering of pleasures and desires, where one part of the self masters the other.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between civic courage and animal/slave courage is important: Plato values educated virtue, not mere toughness -- Cross-ref: Laches where different kinds of courage are distinguished -- The pivot to temperance (sophrosyne) introduces one of the most important Greek ethical concepts -- 'Master of oneself' (kreitto hautou) was a common Greek expression -- The paradox of self-mastery implies that the self has parts -- this is the first hint of the tripartite soul theory that will follow -- Cross-ref: Charmides, Plato's dialogue on temperance -- Temperance as 'harmony' differentiates it from wisdom and courage, which each reside in one part

narrative:
"Certainly."

"The meaning is, I believe, that in the human soul there is a better and also a worse principle; and when the better has the worse under control, then a man is said to be master of himself; and this is a term of praise: but when, owing to evil education or association, the better principle, which is also the smaller, is overwhelmed by the greater mass of the worse -- in this case he is blamed and is called the slave of self and unprincipled."
refashioned:
"Certainly."

"Here's what I think the expression means: in the human soul, there's a better part and a worse part. When the better part controls the worse, a person is called 'master of himself' -- a term of praise. But when bad education or bad company allows the worse part, which is also the larger, to overwhelm the better part, which is smaller -- then the person is blamed and called 'a slave to himself' and 'unprincipled.'"
elegant: The soul has a better and a worse principle. When the better controls the worse, a person is praised as 'master of himself.' When bad education lets the worse (larger) part overwhelm the better (smaller) part, the person is called a slave to self and unprincipled.
summary: The expression 'master of oneself' means that the better part of the soul controls the worse. When bad education or association lets the worse part -- which is larger -- overwhelm the smaller better part, a person becomes a slave to their own appetites.
distilled: Self-mastery means the soul's better but smaller principle controls its worse but larger one; bad education reverses this natural order.
notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- This is a crucial passage establishing that the soul has distinct parts with a natural hierarchy -- The 'better principle' is reason, the 'worse' is appetite -- The observation that the better is also the smaller anticipates the tripartite soul: reason is the smallest part, appetite the largest -- Cross-ref: 4.22 where wisdom resides in the smallest class -- Evil education and association as causes of inversion connect back to 4.11-4.14 on education's paramount importance -- 'Slave of self' inverts the praise of self-mastery and anticipates Book IX's portrait of the tyrant as the ultimate slave to appetite

narrative:
"Yes, there is reason in that."

"And now," I said, "look at our newly created State, and there you will find one of these two conditions realised; for the State, as you will acknowledge, may be justly called master of itself, if the words 'temperance' and 'self-mastery' truly express the rule of the better part over the worse."

"Yes," he said, "I see that what you say is true."

"Let me further note that the manifold and complex pleasures and desires and pains are generally found in children and women and servants, and in the freemen so called who are of the lowest and more numerous class."

"Certainly," he said.

"Whereas the simple and moderate desires which follow reason, and are under the guidance of mind and true opinion, are to be found only in a few, and those the best born and best educated."

"Very true."

"These two, as you may perceive, have a place in our State; and the meaner desires of the many are held down by the virtuous desires and wisdom of the few."

"That I perceive," he said.

"Then if there be any city which may be described as master of its own pleasures and desires, and master of itself, ours may claim such a designation?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"It may also be called temperate, and for the same reasons?"

"Yes."

"And if there be any State in which rulers and subjects will be agreed as to the question who are to rule, that again will be our State?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And the citizens being thus agreed among themselves, in which class will temperance be found -- in the rulers or in the subjects?"

"In both, as I should imagine," he replied.

"Do you observe that we were not far wrong in our guess that temperance was a sort of harmony?"
refashioned:
"That makes sense."

"Now look at our city. It realizes this principle perfectly. The city can justly be called 'master of itself' if temperance and self-mastery mean the rule of the better part over the worse."

"I see that's true."

"Notice that complex, unrestrained pleasures and desires are generally found in children, women, servants, and the lowest, most numerous class of so-called freemen."

"Certainly."

"Whereas simple, moderate desires guided by reason and true judgment are found only in a few -- the best born and best educated."

"True."

"Both types exist in our city, but the base desires of the many are kept in check by the virtuous desires and wisdom of the few."

"I can see that."

"So if any city can be called master of its own pleasures and desires -- master of itself -- it's ours."

"Certainly."

"And it can be called temperate for the same reasons?"

"Yes."

"And if there's any city where rulers and subjects agree about who should rule, that's our city too."

"Undoubtedly."

"Since the citizens agree among themselves, in which class does temperance reside -- rulers or subjects?"

"In both, I'd say."

"So we were right to guess that temperance is a kind of harmony."
elegant: The city is master of itself because the better part (the wise few) governs the worse (the appetitive many). Complex desires belong to the many; simple, rational desires to the educated few. Temperance exists in both rulers and subjects as their agreement about who should rule -- it is indeed a kind of harmony pervading the whole city.
summary: The city is self-mastered because the wise few govern the appetitive many. Unrestrained desires belong to the masses; rational, moderate desires belong to the educated few. Temperance uniquely resides in both rulers and subjects, as their shared agreement about who should rule -- making it a harmony that pervades the entire city.
distilled: Temperance is the harmony between rulers and ruled, their shared agreement that the wise should govern -- unlike wisdom and courage, it pervades the whole city.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The attribution of complex desires to 'children, women, servants, and the lowest class' reflects ancient Greek social assumptions -- The key insight is that temperance, unlike wisdom or courage, belongs to the whole city, not just one class -- This makes temperance unique among the virtues as harmony/agreement rather than a quality of one part -- Cross-ref: The musical metaphor of harmony connects to Books II-III on musical education -- The agreement between rulers and ruled about who should rule is a proto-social-contract idea -- Cross-ref: This parallels the soul where temperance means all parts agree that reason should rule

narrative:
"Why so?"

"Why, because temperance is unlike courage and wisdom, each of which resides in a part only, the one making the State wise and the other valiant; not so temperance, which extends to the whole, and runs through all the notes of the scale, and produces a harmony of the weaker and the stronger and the middle class, whether you suppose them to be stronger or weaker in wisdom or power or numbers or wealth, or anything else. Most truly then may we deem temperance to be the agreement of the naturally superior and inferior, as to the right to rule of either, both in states and individuals."
refashioned:
"How is temperance like harmony?"

"Because temperance is unlike courage and wisdom, each of which resides in just one part of the city -- one making it wise, the other courageous. Temperance extends to the whole. It runs through every note of the scale, producing harmony among the weaker, the stronger, and the middle class -- whether you measure strength by wisdom, power, numbers, wealth, or anything else. So temperance is most truly the agreement between the naturally superior and the naturally inferior about the right to rule -- in both cities and individuals."
elegant: Unlike wisdom and courage, which each reside in one class, temperance extends through the entire city like a harmony running through every note. It is the agreement between the naturally superior and inferior about who has the right to rule -- in both states and individuals.
summary: Temperance differs fundamentally from wisdom and courage: while each of those resides in a single class, temperance runs through the whole city like a harmony spanning every note of the scale. It is the agreement between superior and inferior about who should rule, applicable to both states and individuals.
distilled: Temperance is the whole-city harmony -- the agreement between superior and inferior about the right to rule -- spanning every class like every note of a musical scale.
notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- The musical metaphor is fully developed: temperance as harmony across all notes of the scale -- The three classes (weaker, stronger, middle) map to the city's producers, auxiliaries, and guardians -- Cross-ref: Books II-III on musical harmony as a model for the soul and city -- The definition applies to 'both states and individuals,' explicitly bridging the city-soul analogy -- This foreshadows the soul's temperance: all parts agreeing reason should rule -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 47d on cosmic harmony -- The emphasis on 'naturally' superior/inferior reflects Plato's commitment to natural hierarchy based on ability, not birth

narrative:
"I entirely agree with you."

"And so," I said, "we may consider three out of the four virtues to have been discovered in our State. The last of those qualities which make a state virtuous must be justice, if we only knew what that was."

"The inference is obvious."

"The time then has arrived, Glaucon, when, like huntsmen, we should surround the cover, and look sharp that justice does not steal away, and pass out of sight and escape us; for beyond a doubt she is somewhere in this country: watch therefore and strive to catch a sight of her, and if you see her first, let me know."

"Would that I could! but you should regard me rather as a follower who has just eyes enough to see what you show him -- that is about as much as I am good for."

"Offer up a prayer with me and follow."

"I will, but you must show me the way."

"Here is no path," I said, "and the wood is dark and perplexing; still we must push on."

"Let us push on."

"Here I saw something: Halloo!" I said, "I begin to perceive a track, and I believe that the quarry will not escape."
refashioned:
"I completely agree."

"So we've found three of the four virtues in our city. The last one -- justice -- must be whatever remains, if only we can identify it."

"That's the obvious conclusion."

"The time has come, Glaucon. Like hunters, we must surround the thicket and watch carefully so justice doesn't slip away and escape. She's somewhere in this territory -- keep your eyes open, and if you spot her first, tell me."

"I wish I could! But you should think of me as a follower who can barely see what you point out. That's about the best I can do."

"Then pray with me, and follow."

"I'll follow, but you must lead the way."

"There's no clear path here, and the woods are dark and confusing. But we must press on."

"Let's press on."

"Wait -- I see something! I'm picking up a track. I don't think our quarry will escape."
elegant: Three virtues found; justice must be the fourth. Like hunters, they must surround the thicket so justice doesn't escape. The woods are dark and the path unclear, but Socrates picks up a trail.
summary: With wisdom, courage, and temperance identified, Socrates turns to justice -- the quarry they've been hunting all along. Using an extended hunting metaphor, he urges Glaucon to watch carefully lest justice escape. Despite dark, pathless woods, Socrates picks up a trail.
distilled: With three virtues found, Socrates and Glaucon hunt for justice in dark, pathless woods -- and Socrates begins to pick up the trail.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The hunting metaphor (huntsmen, cover, quarry, track) is vivid and sustained -- Glaucon's modesty ('a follower who has just eyes enough to see what you show him') reflects the ideal philosophical student -- 'The wood is dark and perplexing' echoes Dante's 'selva oscura' centuries later -- The personification of justice as female (she/her) is standard in Greek -- 'Offer up a prayer' reminds us the search for justice has a quasi-religious dimension -- Cross-ref: Book I began with the question 'What is justice?' -- this is the climactic moment where the answer begins to emerge -- The dramatic suspense is a literary device characteristic of Plato's dialogues

narrative:
"Good news," he said.

"Truly," I said, "we are stupid fellows."

"Why so?"

"Why, my good sir, at the beginning of our enquiry, ages ago, there was justice tumbling out at our feet, and we never saw her; nothing could be more ridiculous. Like people who go about looking for what they have in their hands -- that was the way with us -- we looked not at what we were seeking, but at what was far off in the distance; and therefore, I suppose, we missed her."
refashioned:
"Good news!"

"You know," I said, "we've been rather stupid."

"How so?"

"From the very beginning of our inquiry, ages ago, justice was tumbling out right at our feet and we never noticed. How ridiculous! We're like people searching everywhere for something they're already holding in their hands. We were looking at things far off in the distance when what we sought was right in front of us. That's why we missed it."
elegant: Justice has been at their feet from the very beginning, but they were looking far off in the distance. Like people searching for what they already hold in their hands, they missed what was right in front of them.
summary: Socrates announces that they've been foolish: justice has been right at their feet since the beginning of the inquiry, but they were looking far off in the distance and missed it -- like people searching for something they already hold in their hands.
distilled: Justice was present from the very start of the inquiry, but they missed it by looking far away instead of at what was right before them.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The image of searching for what you already hold is a powerful epistemological metaphor -- it suggests that justice is not exotic or hidden but obvious once properly seen -- Cross-ref: The Meno's paradox of inquiry (how can you search for what you don't know?) -- Cross-ref: Book VII's cave allegory where truth is discovered by turning around, not by looking further -- 'Ages ago' refers to Book II where the principle of specialization was first established -- The comic self-deprecation ('we are stupid fellows') is characteristic Socratic irony -- This moment of recognition (anagnorisis) is a dramatic technique borrowed from tragedy

narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"I mean to say that in reality for a long time past we have been talking of justice, and have failed to recognise her."

"I grow impatient at the length of your exordium."

"Well then, tell me," I said, "whether I am right or not: You remember the original principle which we were always laying down at the foundation of the State, that one man should practise one thing only, the thing to which his nature was best adapted; -- now justice is this principle or a part of it."

"Yes, we often said that one man should do one thing only."

"Further, we affirmed that justice was doing one's own business, and not being a busybody; we said so again and again, and many others have said the same to us."

"Yes, we said so."

"Then to do one's own business in a certain way may be assumed to be justice. Can you tell me whence I derive this inference?"
refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"We've been talking about justice for ages without recognizing it."

"I'm growing impatient with your buildup."

"Then tell me if I'm right: remember the foundational principle we kept returning to when building our city -- that each person should practice one thing only, the thing their nature is best suited for? Justice is this principle, or at least part of it."

"Yes, we said repeatedly that one person should do one thing."

"And we also said that justice means doing your own business and not meddling in others' -- we said it again and again, and others have told us the same."

"We did say that."

"So doing one's own business, in a certain sense, is justice. Can you see where this inference comes from?"
elegant: Justice is the foundational principle they established from the start: each person should practice the one thing their nature is best suited for. Justice is doing one's own business and not meddling in others' affairs.
summary: Socrates reveals that justice is the foundational principle of specialization they established at the city's founding: each person should practice the one thing their nature is best adapted for. Justice is doing one's own business and not being a busybody -- a principle they've stated repeatedly without recognizing it as justice itself.
distilled: Justice is the principle of specialization -- each person doing the one thing their nature fits them for and not meddling in others' work.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's impatience ('I grow impatient at the length of your exordium') adds dramatic realism -- The definition of justice as 'doing one's own business' (ta hautou prattein) and 'not being a busybody' (me polypragmonein) is the Republic's central thesis -- Cross-ref: Book II (369b-370c) where the specialization principle was first established -- Cross-ref: Book I where various definitions of justice were proposed and rejected -- 'Many others have said the same' may refer to traditional Greek wisdom about minding one's own affairs -- The qualification 'in a certain way' is important: it's not merely doing your job, but doing the work your nature is designed for

"I cannot, but I should like to be told."

"Because I think that this is the only virtue which remains in the State when the other virtues of temperance and courage and wisdom are abstracted; and, that this is the ultimate cause and condition of the existence of all of them, and while remaining in them is also their preservative; and we were saying that if the three were discovered by us, justice would be the fourth or remaining one."
refashioned:
"I can't see it. Please explain."

"Because this is the only virtue that remains after we've accounted for wisdom, courage, and temperance. More than that -- it's the ultimate cause and condition for the existence of all three. While present in each of them, it also preserves them. And we said from the start that once we found the other three, justice would be whatever remained."
elegant: Justice is the remaining virtue once wisdom, courage, and temperance are accounted for. But it is more than a residue -- it is the cause and condition of the other three virtues' existence, and while present in each, it also preserves them all.
summary: Justice is not merely the residual fourth virtue after wisdom, courage, and temperance are identified. It is the ultimate cause and precondition for all three -- present within each of them and serving as their preservative. Without justice (each part doing its own work), no other virtue can exist.
distilled: Justice is both the fourth virtue by elimination and the foundational cause that makes the other three virtues possible and preserves them.
notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- The claim that justice is the 'cause and condition' of the other virtues elevates it beyond being merely the residue of elimination -- Justice becomes the architectonic virtue -- Cross-ref: This echoes Aristotle's later claim (Nicomachean Ethics V.1) that justice is the whole of virtue -- The idea that justice 'remains in' the other virtues while also being distinct anticipates complex metaphysical questions about the unity of virtue -- Cross-ref: The Protagoras on whether virtue is one or many -- The logical method (find three, the fourth is the remainder) is now shown to be somewhat misleading: justice undergirds all the others

narrative:
"That follows of necessity."

"If we are asked to determine which of these four qualities by its presence contributes most to the excellence of the State, whether the agreement of rulers and subjects, or the preservation in the soldiers of the opinion which the law ordains about the true nature of dangers, or wisdom and watchfulness in the rulers, or whether this other which I am mentioning, and which is found in children and women, slave and freeman, artisan, ruler, subject -- the quality, I mean, of every one doing his own work, and not being a busybody, would claim the palm -- the question is not so easily answered."
refashioned:
"That follows necessarily."

"Now if we're asked which of these four qualities contributes most to the city's excellence -- is it the agreement between rulers and subjects (temperance)? The soldiers' preservation of law-taught opinion about danger (courage)? Wisdom and vigilance in the rulers? Or is it this other quality I'm describing, found everywhere -- in children, women, slaves, free citizens, artisans, rulers, and subjects alike -- the quality of everyone doing their own work and not meddling? The question is not easily answered."
elegant: Which virtue contributes most to the city's excellence? Temperance (agreement between rulers and ruled), courage (soldiers preserving right opinion about danger), wisdom (rulers' vigilance), or justice (everyone doing their own work)? Found universally -- in every class and condition -- justice rivals all the others for primacy.
summary: Socrates poses the question of which virtue contributes most to the city's excellence. Justice -- everyone doing their own work -- is found universally across all classes and conditions, from children to rulers. Its claim to primacy rivals that of temperance, courage, and wisdom, and the answer is not straightforward.
distilled: Justice, the quality of everyone doing their own work, pervades every class and condition, making its contribution to the city's excellence rival that of all other virtues.
notes: Speakers: Socrates poses the question to Glaucon -- The summary of all four virtues in terms of their civic manifestations provides a clear overview: temperance = agreement, courage = preservation of opinion, wisdom = rulers' knowledge, justice = specialization -- Justice is notably the most universal: found in every class, age, and status -- 'Children and women, slave and freeman' makes justice uniquely egalitarian among the virtues -- Cross-ref: This universality of justice connects to its role as the foundational virtue in 4.33 -- The reluctance to rank the virtues definitively shows philosophical caution

narrative:
"Certainly," he replied, "there would be a difficulty in saying which."

"Then the power of each individual in the State to do his own work appears to compete with the other political virtues, wisdom, temperance, courage."

"Yes," he said.

"And the virtue which enters into this competition is justice?"

"Exactly."

"Let us look at the question from another point of view: Are not the rulers in a State those to whom you would entrust the office of determining suits at law?"

"Certainly."

"And are suits decided on any other ground but that a man may neither take what is another's, nor be deprived of what is his own?"

"Yes; that is their principle."

"Which is a just principle?"

"Yes."

"Then on this view also justice will be admitted to be the having and doing what is a man's own, and belongs to him?"

"Very true."

"Think, now, and say whether you agree with me or not. Suppose a carpenter to be doing the business of a cobbler, or a cobbler of a carpenter; and suppose them to exchange their implements or their duties, or the same person to be doing the work of both, or whatever be the change; do you think that any great harm would result to the State?"
refashioned:
"That would be hard to say," he replied.

"So each individual's power to do their own work competes with wisdom, temperance, and courage for contributing most to the city's excellence."

"It does."

"And this competing virtue is justice?"

"Exactly."

"Let's approach it from another angle. The rulers are the ones we'd entrust with deciding lawsuits, correct?"

"Certainly."

"And lawsuits are decided on what principle? That no one should take what belongs to another or be deprived of what is their own."

"That's the principle."

"And that's a just principle?"

"Yes."

"So from this angle too, justice means having and doing what is your own -- what belongs to you."

"True."

"Now consider this: if a carpenter does a cobbler's work, or a cobbler does a carpenter's, or they swap tools and duties, or one person tries to do both -- would any great harm come to the city?"
elegant: Justice competes with the other virtues for contributing most to civic excellence. From the perspective of law courts, justice means each person having and doing what is their own. The practical test: if a carpenter and cobbler swap roles, the harm to the city is minimal.
summary: Justice as each person doing their own work competes with the other virtues for importance. From a legal perspective, justice means no one takes another's property or is deprived of their own. Socrates tests the principle: if a carpenter and cobbler swap roles, the harm to the city is slight -- setting up the contrast with more dangerous class violations.
distilled: Justice means having and doing what is one's own; minor role-swaps between craftsmen cause little harm, but the principle has deeper implications.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The legal perspective on justice (each having their own) connects to Cephalus's definition in Book I (331d) about returning what is owed -- The carpenter-cobbler exchange tests a low-stakes case before examining the high-stakes case of class violation -- Cross-ref: Book II (369d-370b) where the division of labor was established -- The 'having and doing' formula combines possessive and active dimensions of justice -- This sets up the crucial argument in 4.36 that while craft-swapping is harmless, class-swapping is catastrophic

narrative:
"Not much."

"But when the cobbler or any other man whom nature designed to be a trader, having his heart lifted up by wealth or strength or the number of his followers, or any like advantage, attempts to force his way into the class of warriors, or a warrior into that of legislators and guardians, for which he is unfitted, and either to take the implements or the duties of the other; or when one man is trader, legislator, and warrior all in one, then I think you will agree with me in saying that this interchange and this meddling of one with another is the ruin of the State."
refashioned:
"Not much harm."

"But when a cobbler or any other person nature designed to be a trader -- emboldened by wealth, strength, popular following, or some similar advantage -- forces their way into the warrior class, or a warrior pushes into the class of legislators and guardians for which they're unfit, taking on roles and responsibilities that aren't theirs; or when one person tries to be trader, legislator, and warrior all at once -- then I think you'll agree that this kind of interchange and meddling is the ruin of the city."
elegant: When those nature designed for commerce force their way into the warrior or guardian class -- driven by wealth, strength, or ambition -- or when one person tries to be trader, legislator, and warrior simultaneously, this meddling across classes is the ruin of the state.
summary: While craftsmen swapping roles causes little harm, when someone nature designed for commerce forces their way into the warrior or guardian class -- driven by wealth, strength, or followers -- or when one person tries to fill all roles simultaneously, this class-meddling is the ruin of the state.
distilled: The ruin of the state comes not from minor craft-swapping but from ambitious individuals forcing their way across fundamental class boundaries.
notes: Speakers: Socrates to Glaucon -- The escalation from harmless craft-swapping (4.35) to catastrophic class-meddling is the key move -- 'Heart lifted up by wealth or strength' describes the ambitious demagogue or tyrant -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's account of how democracy degenerates when unqualified people seize power -- 'Trader, legislator, and warrior all in one' describes the undifferentiated citizen that Plato considers dangerous -- Cross-ref: Book II's principle of specialization -- The three classes (trader, warrior, guardian/legislator) map onto the tripartite soul (appetite, spirit, reason) -- This passage directly defines injustice as class-meddling

narrative:
"Most true."

"Seeing then," I said, "that there are three distinct classes, any meddling of one with another, or the change of one into another, is the greatest harm to the State, and may be most justly termed evil-doing?"

"Precisely."

"And the greatest degree of evil-doing to one's own city would be termed by you injustice?"

"Certainly."

"This then is injustice; and on the other hand when the trader, the auxiliary, and the guardian each do their own business, that is justice, and will make the city just."
refashioned:
"Absolutely true."

"So with three distinct classes, any meddling between them -- any attempt by one class to take over the role of another -- is the greatest harm to the city, and can rightly be called evil-doing."

"Precisely."

"And the worst form of evil-doing against one's own city would be called injustice?"

"Certainly."

"Then this is injustice: classes meddling with each other's roles. And justice is the opposite: when the traders, auxiliaries, and guardians each do their own work. That is what makes the city just."
elegant: Injustice is the meddling of the three classes with one another's roles -- the greatest harm to the city. Justice is the opposite: when traders, auxiliaries, and guardians each do their own business.
summary: Socrates delivers the formal definition: injustice is the meddling of the three classes with one another's roles, the greatest possible harm to the city. Justice is the opposite -- each class performing its own function. This makes the city just.
distilled: Injustice is class-meddling; justice is each class -- traders, auxiliaries, and guardians -- performing its own proper function.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the definitive statement of justice in the city: each of the three classes doing its own work -- Cross-ref: This will be transferred to the individual soul in 4.58-4.62 -- The three classes (traders/producers, auxiliaries/warriors, guardians/rulers) correspond to the three parts of the soul (appetite, spirit, reason) -- Cross-ref: Book II-III's establishment of the three-class system -- 'Evil-doing' (kakourgian) is a strong term, making class-meddling equivalent to criminal behavior -- This definition resolves the question posed in Book I by Thrasymachus, Polemarchus, and Cephalus

narrative:
"I agree with you."

"We will not," I said, "be over-positive as yet; but if, on trial, this conception of justice be verified in the individual as well as in the State, there will be no longer any room for doubt; if it be not verified, we must have a fresh enquiry. First let us complete the old investigation, which we began, as you remember, under the impression that, if we could previously examine justice on the larger scale, there would be less difficulty in discerning her in the individual. That larger example appeared to be the State, and accordingly we constructed as good a one as we could, knowing well that in the good State justice would be found. Let the discovery which we made be now applied to the individual -- if they agree, we shall be satisfied; or, if there be a difference in the individual, we will come back to the State and have another trial of the theory. The friction of the two when rubbed together may possibly strike a light in which justice will shine forth, and the vision which is then revealed we will fix in our souls."
refashioned:
"I agree."

"Let's not be overconfident yet. If this conception of justice holds true for the individual as well as the city, there will be no room for doubt. If it doesn't, we'll need a fresh inquiry. Remember our original plan: we decided to examine justice on the larger scale first -- the city -- because it would be easier to see. So we built the best city we could, confident justice would be found in it. Now let's apply what we've discovered to the individual. If city and individual match, we're satisfied. If not, we'll return to the city and test the theory again. By rubbing the two together, we may strike a spark that reveals justice clearly, and that vision we'll fix permanently in our souls."
elegant: The conception of justice must now be tested in the individual. If city and individual agree, the theory is confirmed. If not, rubbing the two together like flint may strike a spark that illuminates justice clearly enough to fix the vision permanently in their souls.
summary: Socrates cautions against premature certainty. The definition of justice found in the city must now be tested in the individual. If city and soul agree, the theory is confirmed; if not, they'll try again. By comparing the two -- like rubbing flint -- they may strike a spark that illuminates justice clearly enough to fix it permanently in their souls.
distilled: The definition of justice in the city must be verified in the individual soul; comparing the two may strike the spark of true understanding.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'larger letters' analogy from Book II (368d) is recalled here: they examined justice in the city first because it would be easier to see at a larger scale -- The fire-striking metaphor (friction producing light) is a powerful image for dialectical discovery -- Cross-ref: Seventh Letter 344b where Plato describes knowledge flashing forth like fire from rubbed sticks -- 'Fix in our souls' suggests the vision of justice becomes permanent knowledge -- This passage marks the major transition from political to psychological analysis -- The method of comparing city and soul is the Republic's distinctive philosophical strategy

narrative:
"That will be in regular course; let us do as you say."

"I proceeded to ask: When two things, a greater and less, are called by the same name, are they like or unlike in so far as they are called the same?"

"Like," he replied.

"The just man then, if we regard the idea of justice only, will be like the just State?"

"He will."

"And a State was thought by us to be just when the three classes in the State severally did their own business; and also thought to be temperate and valiant and wise by reason of certain other affections and qualities of these same classes?"

"True," he said.

"And so of the individual; we may assume that he has the same three principles in his own soul which are found in the State; and he may be rightly described in the same terms, because he is affected in the same manner?"

"Certainly," he said.

"Once more then, O my friend, we have alighted upon an easy question -- whether the soul has these three principles or not?"

"An easy question! Nay, rather, Socrates, the proverb holds that hard is the good."

"Very true," I said; "and I do not think that the method which we are employing is at all adequate to the accurate solution of this question; the true method is another and a longer one. Still we may arrive at a solution not below the level of the previous enquiry."

"May we not be satisfied with that?" he said; "-- under the circumstances, I am quite content."
refashioned:
"Let's proceed as you suggest."

"When two things, one larger and one smaller, share the same name, are they alike insofar as they share that name?"

"They are."

"Then the just person, considered purely in terms of justice, will resemble the just city."

"Yes."

"We said the city is just when its three classes each do their own work, and wise, courageous, and temperate because of certain qualities in those same classes."

"True."

"So the individual must have the same three principles in their soul as exist in the city, and can be described in the same terms for the same reasons."

"Certainly."

"So here's another easy question: does the soul actually have these three principles?"

"Easy? Hardly, Socrates! The proverb says 'the good is hard.'"

"You're right. And I'll admit that the method we're using isn't really adequate for a precise answer to this question -- the true method would be longer and more rigorous. But we can reach an answer that matches the level of our previous inquiry."

"That's good enough for me, given the circumstances."
elegant: If the just person resembles the just city, the soul must contain the same three principles found in the three classes of the city. But proving the soul has three parts is not easy -- 'the good is hard.' The method here is not fully adequate; a longer, more rigorous approach would be needed for precision. Still, a satisfactory answer is within reach.
summary: Socrates establishes the key analogy: the just person must resemble the just city, so the soul must contain three principles matching the city's three classes. But proving this is harder than it seems. Socrates acknowledges their method is inadequate for full precision -- a longer approach would be needed -- but they can reach a satisfactory answer.
distilled: The soul must have three principles matching the city's three classes, but proving this requires more rigorous methods than they will employ here.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Hard is the good' (chalepa ta kala) is a well-known Greek proverb -- Socrates' admission that 'the true method is another and a longer one' is significant: he's acknowledging that the tripartite soul argument here is preliminary -- Cross-ref: The Phaedrus (246a-249d) and Timaeus (69c-72d) give more elaborate accounts of the soul's parts -- The 'larger and smaller sharing the same name' argument relies on the assumption that city and soul are structurally analogous -- This assumption is fundamental to the Republic but is philosophically debatable -- Cross-ref: Aristotle (Politics I.2) questions whether the analogy between city and soul is valid

narrative:
"I too," I replied, "shall be extremely well satisfied."

"Then faint not in pursuing the speculation," he said.

"Must we not acknowledge," I said, "that in each of us there are the same principles and habits which there are in the State; and that from the individual they pass into the State? -- how else can they come there? Take the quality of passion or spirit; -- it would be ridiculous to imagine that this quality, when found in States, is not derived from the individuals who are supposed to possess it, e.g. the Thracians, Scythians, and in general the northern nations; and the same may be said of the love of knowledge, which is the special characteristic of our part of the world, or of the love of money, which may, with equal truth, be attributed to the Phoenicians and Egyptians."
refashioned:
"I'll be satisfied too."

"Then don't lose heart -- keep pursuing the argument," he said.

"We must acknowledge that the same principles and dispositions found in the city exist first in its individual citizens -- where else would the city get them? Take spirit or passion: it would be absurd to claim this quality in certain states doesn't come from their individual people. The Thracians, Scythians, and northern peoples generally are known for their spiritedness. The love of knowledge is the special characteristic of our Greek world. The love of money can be attributed to the Phoenicians and Egyptians."
elegant: The qualities found in a city derive from its individual citizens. Spiritedness characterizes northern peoples (Thracians, Scythians), love of knowledge characterizes Greeks, and love of money characterizes Phoenicians and Egyptians. The same principles that structure the city must first exist in the individual soul.
summary: Socrates argues that a city's character derives from its individual citizens, not the reverse. He illustrates with national stereotypes: Thracians and Scythians are spirited, Greeks love knowledge, Phoenicians and Egyptians love money. These three qualities in different peoples confirm that the same three principles exist in individuals.
distilled: The city's character comes from its citizens; the spiritedness, love of knowledge, and love of money found in different nations prove these principles exist in individual souls.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The national stereotypes (Thracian spirit, Greek reason, Phoenician commerce) were commonplace in Greek ethnography -- The three national characters map onto the three parts of the soul: spirit (thymos), reason (logos), appetite (epithymia) -- Cross-ref: Herodotus and other Greek historians who characterized peoples by dominant traits -- The argument that the city's character derives from individuals, not vice versa, is an important methodological claim -- 'Our part of the world' means Greece, specifically Athens as the philosophical center -- The Phoenicians were the great traders of the ancient Mediterranean -- This argument uses empirical observation (national character) to support the tripartite soul theory

narrative:
"Exactly so," he said.

"There is no difficulty in understanding this."

"None whatever."

"But the question is not quite so easy when we proceed to ask whether these principles are three or one; whether, that is to say, we learn with one part of our nature, are angry with another, and with a third part desire the satisfaction of our natural appetites; or whether the whole soul comes into play in each sort of action -- to determine that is the difficulty."
refashioned:
"Exactly," he said.

"That's easy enough to understand."

"No difficulty at all."

"But here's where it gets harder: are these principles actually three distinct parts, or one? Do we learn with one part of our nature, get angry with another, and desire with a third? Or does the whole soul engage in each type of activity? Determining that is the real difficulty."
elegant: The real difficulty: are reason, spirit, and appetite three distinct parts of the soul, or does the whole soul engage in each activity? Do we learn with one part, get angry with another, and desire with a third?
summary: The easy part is recognizing that the same qualities exist in individuals and cities. The hard question is whether these are three distinct parts of the soul or one: do we reason with one part, feel anger with another, and desire with a third? Or does the whole soul participate in every action?
distilled: The crucial question is whether reason, spirit, and appetite are three distinct parts of the soul or whether the whole soul engages in each activity.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the formal statement of the problem that the tripartite soul theory will answer -- The three activities (learning, anger, appetite) correspond to the three proposed parts (reason, spirit, appetite) -- Cross-ref: The Phaedrus chariot allegory (246a-254e) presents the same tripartition as charioteer, noble horse, and base horse -- Cross-ref: De Anima where Aristotle critiques the idea of soul-parts -- The question 'three or one' has vast implications for ethics, psychology, and the possibility of inner conflict -- Plato will use the principle of non-contradiction (same thing cannot do opposites simultaneously in the same respect) to argue for distinct parts

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "there lies the difficulty."

"Then let us now try and determine whether they are the same or different."

"How can we?" he asked.

"I replied as follows: The same thing clearly cannot act or be acted upon in the same part or in relation to the same thing at the same time, in contrary ways; and therefore whenever this contradiction occurs in things apparently the same, we know that they are really not the same, but different."

"Good."

"For example," I said, "can the same thing be at rest and in motion at the same time in the same part?"
refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly where the difficulty lies."

"Then let's try to determine whether these are the same or different."

"How?"

"Here's the principle: the same thing clearly cannot act or be acted upon in contrary ways, at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing. So whenever we find this contradiction in something that appears to be one, we know it's actually not one but many."

"Good principle."

"For instance: can the same thing be at rest and in motion at the same time, in the same part of itself?"
elegant: The key principle: the same thing cannot act in contrary ways at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing. When contradictory impulses appear in a single entity, that entity must contain distinct parts.
summary: Socrates introduces the foundational logical principle: the same thing cannot act or be acted upon in contrary ways simultaneously, in the same respect, regarding the same thing. Whenever apparent contradiction occurs in what seems to be one thing, that thing must actually contain distinct parts.
distilled: The principle of non-contradiction -- the same thing cannot do opposite things at the same time in the same respect -- will prove the soul has distinct parts.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is Plato's statement of the principle of non-contradiction, one of the most important logical principles in Western philosophy -- Cross-ref: Aristotle formalized this as the Law of Non-Contradiction in Metaphysics IV.3 -- The qualifications 'same part,' 'same time,' 'same relation' are crucial: they prevent easy counterexamples -- The principle will be applied to the soul: if the soul simultaneously desires and rejects the same thing, it must have distinct parts -- Cross-ref: Physics I where Aristotle discusses motion and rest -- This passage establishes the logical foundation for everything that follows in the tripartite soul argument

narrative:
"Impossible."

"Still," I said, "let us have a more precise statement of terms, lest we should hereafter fall out by the way. Imagine the case of a man who is standing and also moving his hands and his head, and suppose a person to say that one and the same person is in motion and at rest at the same moment -- to such a mode of speech we should object, and should rather say that one part of him is in motion while another is at rest."
refashioned:
"Impossible."

"But let's be more precise, so we don't run into trouble later. Imagine someone standing still while moving their hands and head. If someone said this person is simultaneously in motion and at rest, we'd object. We'd say one part of them is moving while another part is still."
elegant: Precision is needed: a person standing still while moving their hands is not simultaneously in motion and at rest. One part is moving, another is still. The principle of non-contradiction must be applied to the same part.
summary: Socrates insists on precision: when a person stands still while moving their hands, they are not contradicting the principle. One part of them is moving while another is at rest. The principle of non-contradiction applies to the same part, not the whole person.
distilled: Non-contradiction must be precisely applied: different parts of the same thing can be in different states simultaneously without contradiction.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The example of standing while moving hands is an everyday illustration of the part/whole distinction -- This precision is essential: without it, any complex entity would trivially violate non-contradiction -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's more rigorous treatment in Physics VI on parts and wholes in motion -- The insistence on precise terms ('lest we should hereafter fall out by the way') shows Socratic care with definitions -- This sets up the spinning top example that follows

narrative:
"Very true."

"And suppose the objector to refine still further, and to draw the nice distinction that not only parts of tops, but whole tops, when they spin round with their pegs fixed on the spot, are at rest and in motion at the same time (and he may say the same of anything which revolves in the same spot), his objection would not be admitted by us, because in such cases things are not at rest and in motion in the same parts of themselves; we should rather say that they have both an axis and a circumference, and that the axis stands still, for there is no deviation from the perpendicular; and that the circumference goes round. But if, while revolving, the axis inclines either to the right or left, forwards or backwards, then in no point of view can they be at rest."
refashioned:
"True."

"And if a clever objector pressed further, arguing that a spinning top with its peg fixed in one spot is simultaneously at rest and in motion -- and the same could be said of anything revolving on a fixed point -- we wouldn't accept that objection. Because in these cases, rest and motion don't apply to the same parts. The top has an axis and a circumference: the axis stands still (there's no deviation from the vertical), while the circumference spins. But if the axis tilts in any direction while the top revolves, then it isn't at rest in any sense."
elegant: A spinning top is not simultaneously at rest and in motion in the same respect: its axis is still while its circumference rotates. But if the axis tilts, nothing is at rest. The principle of non-contradiction holds when properly applied to parts.
summary: Socrates addresses the spinning top objection: a top spinning on a fixed point seems both at rest and in motion, but actually its axis is still while its circumference rotates -- different parts are in different states. If the axis tilts, nothing is at rest. Properly applied, non-contradiction holds.
distilled: A spinning top resolves the apparent contradiction: its axis rests while its circumference moves -- the same thing is not at rest and in motion in the same part.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The spinning top (or gyroscope) example is a famous passage in the history of physics and philosophy -- The distinction between axis and circumference resolves the apparent paradox through part-analysis -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Physics VIII.8 discusses circular motion similarly -- 'Deviation from the perpendicular' is a precise geometric description -- This example demonstrates that apparent violations of non-contradiction can always be resolved by identifying the relevant parts or aspects -- The intellectual rigor here is characteristic of Plato's middle-period metaphysics

narrative:
"That is the correct mode of describing them," he replied.

"Then none of these objections will confuse us, or incline us to believe that the same thing at the same time, in the same part or in relation to the same thing, can act or be acted upon in contrary ways."

"Certainly not, according to my way of thinking."

"Yet," I said, "that we may not be compelled to examine all such objections, and prove at length that they are untrue, let us assume their absurdity, and go forward on the understanding that hereafter, if this assumption turn out to be untrue, all the consequences which follow shall be withdrawn."

"Yes," he said, "that will be the best way."

"Well," I said, "would you not allow that assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion, are all of them opposites, whether they are regarded as active or passive (for that makes no difference in the fact of their opposition)?"
refashioned:
"That's the right way to describe it."

"Good. Then none of these objections will confuse us or make us doubt that the same thing cannot act in contrary ways at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing."

"Not from my perspective."

"Still, rather than addressing every possible objection at length, let's proceed on this assumption. If it turns out to be wrong, we'll retract everything that follows from it."

"That's the best approach."

"Now: would you agree that assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion are all pairs of opposites, whether we consider them as active or passive? That distinction doesn't affect the fact of their opposition."
elegant: The principle of non-contradiction stands firm. Moving forward on this basis: assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion are genuine opposites, whether active or passive.
summary: Having established the principle of non-contradiction, Socrates proposes to assume its truth and proceed, promising to retract conclusions if the assumption fails. He then identifies the key psychological opposites: assent/dissent, desire/aversion, attraction/repulsion -- all genuine pairs of contraries regardless of whether they're active or passive.
distilled: With non-contradiction established, Socrates identifies the psychological opposites -- desire and aversion, assent and dissent, attraction and repulsion -- that will prove the soul's division.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The conditional assumption ('if this turns out untrue, we'll retract') shows Platonic philosophical honesty -- Moving from physical examples (tops, standing man) to psychological opposites (desire/aversion) bridges the transition to soul analysis -- The active/passive distinction is a subtle philosophical point: wanting and being drawn to something are both forms of desire -- Cross-ref: These psychological opposites will be used in 4.46-4.51 to demonstrate the soul has distinct parts -- The list of opposites (assent/dissent, desire/aversion, attraction/repulsion) covers cognitive, conative, and emotional domains

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "they are opposites."

"Well," I said, "and hunger and thirst, and the desires in general, and again willing and wishing -- all these you would refer to the classes already mentioned. You would say -- would you not? -- that the soul of him who desires is seeking after the object of his desires; or that he is drawing to himself the thing which he wishes to possess: or again, when a person wants anything to be given him, his mind, longing for the realisation of his desires, intimates his wish to have it by a nod of assent, as if he had been asked a question?"
refashioned:
"Yes, they are opposites."

"Now, hunger, thirst, and desires generally -- as well as willing and wishing -- all belong to the category of attraction and assent. When someone desires something, their soul reaches toward the object of desire, drawing it to themselves. When someone wants something given to them, their mind signals its longing with a kind of inner nod of assent, as if answering 'yes' to an unspoken question."
elegant: Hunger, thirst, and all desires are forms of attraction: the soul reaches toward what it wants, drawing objects to itself. Desire is fundamentally an assent -- the soul nodding 'yes' to its objects.
summary: Socrates classifies hunger, thirst, and all desires as forms of attraction and assent. When the soul desires, it reaches toward its object, drawing it inward. Desire functions like a nod of assent -- the soul saying 'yes' to what it wants.
distilled: All desire is a form of attraction and assent -- the soul reaching toward and affirming its objects.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Hunger and thirst are chosen as paradigmatic desires because they are the simplest and most obviously bodily -- The metaphor of desire as a 'nod of assent' connects appetitive and rational language -- Cross-ref: This analysis of desire anticipates 4.48's detailed examination of thirst as 'pure' desire -- The idea of the soul 'drawing to itself' its objects has Platonic metaphysical implications about the relationship between soul and world -- Cross-ref: Symposium 200a-201c where desire is analyzed as desire for what one lacks

narrative:
"Very true."

"And what would you say of unwillingness and dislike and the absence of desire; should not these be referred to the opposite class of repulsion and rejection?"

"Certainly."

"Admitting this to be true of desire generally, let us suppose a particular class of desires, and out of these we will select hunger and thirst, as they are termed, which are the most obvious of them?"
refashioned:
"True."

"And unwillingness, dislike, the absence of desire -- wouldn't these all belong to the opposite category of repulsion and rejection?"

"Certainly."

"If this holds for desire generally, let's examine a specific class. We'll select hunger and thirst, the most obvious examples."
elegant: Unwillingness and dislike are forms of repulsion, the opposite of desire's attraction. To test the theory, hunger and thirst are selected as the most obvious specific desires.
summary: Socrates establishes that unwillingness and dislike belong to the opposite category of repulsion and rejection. To examine desire more precisely, he selects hunger and thirst as the clearest, most obvious examples.
distilled: Desire attracts, aversion repels; hunger and thirst provide the clearest test cases for analyzing how desire works in the soul.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The opposition between desire (attraction) and aversion (repulsion) sets up the key argument: if the soul simultaneously desires and rejects the same thing, it must have parts -- Hunger and thirst are strategically chosen: they are undeniably bodily appetites, making them the clearest cases of 'pure' desire -- Cross-ref: 4.48 will analyze thirst as desire for drink 'pure and simple' -- This methodological move from general to specific is characteristic of Platonic dialectic -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a also analyzes hunger and thirst

narrative:
"Let us take that class," he said.

"The object of one is food, and of the other drink?"

"Yes."

"And here comes the point: is not thirst the desire which the soul has of drink, and of drink only; not of drink qualified by anything else; for example, warm or cold, or much or little, or, in a word, drink of any particular sort: but if the thirst be accompanied by heat, then the desire is of cold drink; or, if accompanied by cold, then of warm drink; or, if the thirst be excessive, then the drink which is desired will be excessive; or, if not great, the quantity of drink will also be small: but thirst pure and simple will desire drink pure and simple, which is the natural satisfaction of thirst, as food is of hunger?"
refashioned:
"Let's examine those."

"Hunger desires food, thirst desires drink?"

"Yes."

"Now here's the crucial point: isn't thirst simply the desire for drink -- drink and nothing more? Not warm drink or cold drink, not a lot or a little, not any particular kind. If the thirst is accompanied by heat, then the desire becomes specific -- cold drink. If accompanied by cold, then warm drink. If the thirst is extreme, the desire is for a large quantity; if mild, for a small amount. But thirst in itself -- pure, unqualified thirst -- desires nothing but drink in itself. Just as hunger in itself desires nothing but food."
elegant: Thirst in itself is simply the desire for drink, unqualified. Only additional conditions (heat, cold, excess) make the desire specific. Pure thirst desires pure drink; pure hunger desires pure food. The basic desire is for its basic object, nothing more.
summary: Socrates makes a crucial distinction: thirst in its pure form is desire for drink and nothing else -- not warm, cold, much, or little. Only accompanying conditions (heat, cold, excess) qualify the desire further. Pure thirst desires pure drink, just as pure hunger desires pure food.
distilled: Pure thirst is desire for drink and nothing else; qualifications arise only from additional conditions, not from thirst itself.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This analysis of 'pure' desire versus 'qualified' desire is a key philosophical move -- It establishes that basic appetites are for basic objects, without rational qualification -- Cross-ref: 4.49 where an objector might say we always desire 'good' drink -- The distinction between unqualified and qualified desire maps onto the distinction between appetite (which just wants) and reason (which evaluates) -- This passage is foundational for Plato's psychology: the appetitive part desires blindly; only reason adds evaluation -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's De Anima III.10 on the objects of desire -- The food/drink analysis anticipates modern philosophy of mind discussions about the intentionality of desire

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "the simple desire is, as you say, in every case of the simple object, and the qualified desire of the qualified object."

"But here a confusion may arise; and I should wish to guard against an opponent starting up and saying that no man desires drink only, but good drink, or food only, but good food; for good is the universal object of desire, and thirst being a desire, will necessarily be thirst after good drink; and the same is true of every other desire."

"Yes," he replied, "the opponent might have something to say."

"Nevertheless I should still maintain, that of relatives some have a quality attached to either term of the relation; others are simple and have their correlatives simple."

"I do not know what you mean."

"Well, you know of course that the greater is relative to the less?"

"Certainly."

"And the much greater to the much less?"

"Yes."

"And the sometime greater to the sometime less, and the greater that is to be to the less that is to be?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And so of more and less, and of other correlative terms, such as the double and the half, or again, the heavier and the lighter, the swifter and the slower; and of hot and cold, and of any other relatives; -- is not this true of all of them?"

"Yes."

"And does not the same principle hold in the sciences? The object of science is knowledge (assuming that to be the true definition), but the object of a particular science is a particular kind of knowledge; I mean, for example, that the science of house-building is a kind of knowledge which is defined and distinguished from other kinds and is therefore termed architecture."

"Certainly."

"Because it has a particular quality which no other has?"

"Yes."

"And it has this particular quality because it has an object of a particular kind; and this is true of the other arts and sciences?"
refashioned:
"Yes -- simple desire for a simple object, qualified desire for a qualified object."

"But a confusion might arise. An opponent could argue that no one ever desires just drink, but good drink; not just food, but good food. Since 'the good' is the universal object of all desire, thirst must really be thirst for good drink -- and the same for every desire."

"That opponent might have a point."

"Still, I maintain this: in relative terms, some have qualities attached to both sides of the relation, while others are simple with simple correlatives."

"I don't follow."

"You know that 'greater' is relative to 'less'?"

"Of course."

"And 'much greater' to 'much less'?"

"Yes."

"And 'sometimes greater' to 'sometimes less,' and 'going to be greater' to 'going to be less'?"

"Yes."

"The same holds for more and less, double and half, heavier and lighter, swifter and slower, hot and cold, and all other relative terms."

"Yes."

"And the same principle applies to the sciences. Science in general has knowledge as its object. But a particular science has a particular kind of knowledge as its object. Architecture, for instance, is defined and distinguished from other sciences because it's the science of house-building."

"Right."

"It has a unique character because it has a unique object. And this holds for all arts and sciences."
elegant: An objector might claim all desire is really desire for the good, making thirst always desire for good drink. But relative terms show the principle: simple terms have simple correlatives, qualified terms have qualified correlatives. 'Greater' is relative to 'less,' 'much greater' to 'much less.' Similarly in science: science in general seeks knowledge, but particular sciences seek particular knowledge -- architecture is defined by its particular object, house-building.
summary: Socrates anticipates the objection that all desire is really for 'the good' (making thirst desire for good drink). He refutes this through the logic of relative terms: simple terms have simple correlatives, qualified have qualified. Just as 'greater' relates to 'less' and 'much greater' to 'much less,' science in general relates to knowledge in general, while architecture relates specifically to house-building knowledge.
distilled: Simple relatives have simple correlatives; just as 'greater' relates to 'less' and general science to general knowledge, pure thirst relates to pure drink -- not 'good' drink.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The objection that all desire is for 'the good' is a Socratic position from earlier dialogues (Meno 77b-78b) -- Here Plato is refining beyond his earlier view: appetite desires its object regardless of goodness -- The theory of relative terms (pros ti) is an important piece of Platonic logic -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Categories 7 develops the theory of relatives more systematically -- The architecture example shows how the object determines the nature of the knowledge -- This entire argument serves to keep 'pure' appetite separate from rational evaluation, supporting the tripartite soul theory

narrative: "Now, then, if I have made myself clear, you will understand my original meaning in what I said about relatives. My meaning was, that if one term of a relation is taken alone, the other is taken alone; if one term is qualified, the other is also qualified. I do not mean to say that relatives may not be disparate, or that the science of health is healthy, or of disease necessarily diseased, or that the sciences of good and evil are therefore good and evil; but only that, when the term science is no longer used absolutely, but has a qualified object which in this case is the nature of health and disease, it becomes defined, and is hence called not merely science, but the science of medicine."
refashioned: "If I've been clear, you now understand what I meant about relatives. My point is: when one term of a relation is taken unqualified, the other is also unqualified; when one is qualified, the other is too. I'm not saying that relatives must be alike in every way -- the science of health isn't itself healthy, and the science of disease isn't diseased; sciences of good and evil aren't themselves good or evil. My point is only that when 'science' is no longer used in its general sense but takes a specific object -- say, health and disease -- it becomes defined, and we call it not just 'science' but 'the science of medicine.'"
elegant: The principle of correlatives: unqualified terms relate to unqualified terms, qualified to qualified. This doesn't mean relatives share their qualities -- medical science isn't itself healthy or sick. It means that when a general term (science) takes a specific object (health), it becomes a specific term (medicine).
summary: Socrates clarifies his principle of correlatives: unqualified terms relate to unqualified terms, qualified to qualified. This doesn't mean they share qualities -- medical science isn't itself healthy. It means when a general term takes a specific object, it becomes a specific discipline. Applied to desire: unqualified thirst relates to unqualified drink.
distilled: Unqualified terms have unqualified correlatives, qualified have qualified -- so pure thirst relates to pure drink, not to 'good' or 'bad' drink.
notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- The clarification that 'the science of health is not healthy' prevents a common fallacy in reasoning about relatives -- This is an important logical point: the relation between knower and known, desirer and desired, doesn't transfer qualities between the terms -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Categories 7 and Topics I make similar distinctions about relatives -- The medical science example is well-chosen because Plato frequently uses medicine as an analogy -- The upshot for psychology: thirst-in-itself relates to drink-in-itself; only additional rational qualifications produce desire for 'good' drink

narrative:
"I quite understand, and I think as you do."

"Would you not say that thirst is one of these essentially relative terms, having clearly a relation --"

"Yes, thirst is relative to drink."

"And a certain kind of thirst is relative to a certain kind of drink; but thirst taken alone is neither of much nor little, nor of good nor bad, nor of any particular kind of drink, but of drink only?"

"Certainly."

"Then the soul of the thirsty one, in so far as he is thirsty, desires only drink; for this he yearns and tries to obtain it?"

"That is plain."

"And if you suppose something which pulls a thirsty soul away from drink, that must be different from the thirsty principle which draws him like a beast to drink; for, as we were saying, the same thing cannot at the same time with the same part of itself act in contrary ways about the same."

"Impossible."

"No more than you can say that the hands of the archer push and pull the bow at the same time, but what you say is that one hand pushes and the other pulls."

"Exactly so," he replied.

"And might a man be thirsty, and yet unwilling to drink?"

"Yes," he said, "it constantly happens."

"And in such a case what is one to say? Would you not say that there was something in the soul bidding a man to drink, and something else forbidding him, which is other and stronger than the principle which bids him?"

"I should say so."

"And the forbidding principle is derived from reason, and that which bids and attracts proceeds from passion and disease?"

"Clearly."

"Then we may fairly assume that they are two, and that they differ from one another; the one with which a man reasons, we may call the rational principle of the soul, the other, with which he loves and hungers and thirsts and feels the flutterings of any other desire, may be termed the irrational or appetitive, the ally of sundry pleasures and satisfactions?"

"Yes," he said, "we may fairly assume them to be different."

"Then let us finally determine that there are two principles existing in the soul. And what of passion, or spirit? Is it a third, or akin to one of the preceding?"
refashioned:
"I understand completely and agree."

"Thirst is one of these relative terms -- clearly related to..."

"Drink. Yes."

"And a particular kind of thirst relates to a particular kind of drink. But thirst in itself is neither for much nor little, good nor bad, nor any specific kind -- just drink."

"Right."

"So the soul of a thirsty person, insofar as they're thirsty, desires only drink. That's what they crave and try to obtain."

"Obviously."

"But if something pulls a thirsty person away from drink, that must be different from the thirsty impulse that draws them to drink like an animal. As we established, the same thing cannot simultaneously act in contrary ways about the same object with the same part of itself."

"Impossible."

"Just as you can't say the archer's hands push and pull the bow at once -- one hand pushes while the other pulls."

"Exactly."

"Can a person be thirsty yet unwilling to drink?"

"Yes, that happens all the time."

"Then what do we say? There must be something in the soul urging the person to drink, and something else forbidding it -- something different and stronger than the urging impulse."

"There must be."

"The forbidding comes from reason; the urging and attracting comes from appetite and passion."

"Clearly."

"So we can fairly conclude these are two distinct principles: the one we reason with, we'll call the rational part of the soul. The other, with which we love, hunger, thirst, and feel the fluttering of every desire, we'll call the irrational or appetitive part -- the ally of various pleasures and satisfactions."

"Yes, we can fairly call them different."

"So two principles are established. But what about passion or spirit? Is it a third, or does it belong to one of these two?"
elegant: Thirst in itself desires only drink. But a person can be thirsty yet refuse to drink -- something in the soul urges while something else forbids. Since the same thing cannot simultaneously do opposites in the same respect, these must be two distinct principles: reason (which forbids) and appetite (which urges). The rational part reasons; the appetitive part hungers, thirsts, and desires. But is spirit a third part, or merely a form of one of these two?
summary: The proof of the soul's division: thirst desires only drink, yet a person can be thirsty and refuse to drink. Since the same thing cannot simultaneously do opposites, two principles must exist: reason (which forbids) and appetite (which urges). The rational part reasons; the appetitive part desires. This establishes two parts of the soul -- but the question remains: is spirit/passion a third part, or does it belong to one of these two?
