distilled: A thirsty person who refuses to drink proves the soul has at least two parts -- reason that forbids and appetite that urges -- and the question arises whether spirit is a third.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the central argument for the bipartite (and eventually tripartite) soul -- The thirsty person refusing to drink is the key empirical observation -- The archer analogy (one hand pushes, the other pulls) brilliantly illustrates how one entity can contain opposing forces -- 'Draws him like a beast to drink' characterizes appetite as animal-like -- 'Flutterings of desire' is vivid psychological language -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-254e develops the soul's parts as charioteer and two horses -- The question about spirit (thymos) sets up 4.52-4.58 -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's De Anima III.9-10 on the desiring and reasoning parts of the soul -- 'Irrational or appetitive' (alogiston kai epithymetikon) becomes standard terminology in Greek philosophy

narrative:
"I should be inclined to say -- akin to desire."

"Well," I said, "there is a story which I remember to have heard, and in which I put faith. The story is, that Leontius, the son of Aglaion, coming up one day from the Piraeus, under the north wall on the outside, observed some dead bodies lying on the ground at the place of execution. He felt a desire to see them, and also a dread and abhorrence of them; for a time he struggled and covered his eyes, but at length the desire got the better of him; and forcing them open, he ran up to the dead bodies, saying, 'Look, ye wretches, take your fill of the fair sight.'"

"I have heard the story myself," he said.

"The moral of the tale is, that anger at times goes to war with desire, as though they were two distinct things."
refashioned:
"I'd be inclined to say spirit is akin to desire."

"Let me tell you a story I believe to be true. Leontius, the son of Aglaion, was walking up from the Piraeus along the outside of the north wall when he noticed corpses lying at the place of execution. He felt a desire to look at them, but also dread and revulsion. For a while he struggled, covering his eyes. But finally the desire overpowered him. He forced his eyes open, ran toward the bodies, and cried out: 'Look, you wretches -- feast your eyes on this fine spectacle!'"

"I've heard that story too."

"The lesson is that anger sometimes goes to war against desire, as if they were two distinct things."
elegant: Leontius felt desire to look at corpses and revulsion that forbade it. When desire won, he cursed his own eyes in anger. The moral: anger (spirit) wars against desire as a separate force. Spirit is not akin to appetite -- it opposes it.
summary: Glaucon suggests spirit is akin to desire, but Socrates tells the story of Leontius: passing corpses at an execution site, he felt both desire to look and revulsion. When desire overpowered him, he cursed his own eyes in anger. The moral: anger (spirit) fights against desire as a distinct force, not an ally.
distilled: The story of Leontius proves that spirit (anger at oneself) wars against desire as a separate force, not as another form of appetite.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The story of Leontius is one of the most famous passages in the Republic and in the history of psychology -- Piraeus was Athens' port city; the north wall was part of the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus -- The 'place of execution' was likely near the Barathron, a pit where condemned criminals were thrown -- Leontius's self-address ('Look, ye wretches') shows spirit (anger at himself) treating his eyes/desires as separate entities -- This is a vivid case of inner conflict: appetite pulls toward the corpses, spirit recoils -- Cross-ref: Freud's concept of the superego punishing the id bears structural similarity -- The story is the key evidence that spirit is not appetite -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 253c-254e where the dark horse (appetite) is restrained by the charioteer and noble horse (reason and spirit)

narrative:
"Yes; that is the meaning," he said.

"And are there not many other cases in which we observe that when a man's desires violently prevail over his reason, he reviles himself, and is angry at the violence within him, and that in this struggle, which is like the struggle of factions in a State, his spirit is on the side of his reason; -- but for the passionate or spirited element to take part with the desires when reason decides that she should not be opposed, is a sort of thing which I believe that you never observed occurring in yourself, nor, as I should imagine, in any one else?"
refashioned:
"Yes, that's the meaning."

"And aren't there many other cases where a person's desires violently overpower their reason, and they curse themselves, angry at the violence within? In this inner struggle -- like a civil war between factions in a city -- spirit always sides with reason. But have you ever observed spirit siding with the desires against reason's judgment? I don't think you've ever seen that in yourself or in anyone else."
elegant: When desires violently overpower reason, a person reviles themselves -- spirit always sides with reason in this inner civil war. Spirit never allies with appetite against reason's judgment. This is a universal observation.
summary: In cases of inner conflict where desires overpower reason, people revile themselves -- their spirit sides with reason, angry at the appetitive violence within. Socrates claims this is universal: spirit never takes the side of desire against reason's judgment, functioning like a political faction that always allies with the ruling class.
distilled: Spirit always sides with reason against desire in inner conflict; it never allies with appetite against reason's judgment.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The inner conflict as 'struggle of factions in a State' (stasis) explicitly connects the soul's structure to political structure -- Spirit's alliance with reason against appetite is the key structural claim of the tripartite theory -- Cross-ref: In the city, the auxiliaries (spirit) support the guardians (reason) against the producers (appetite) -- The claim that spirit never sides with appetite against reason is strong and arguably overdrawn -- Cross-ref: Book IX's analysis of the tyrannical soul where all parts are in disorder -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2 on anger (orge) as a response to perceived injustice -- The self-reviling person shows spirit as a kind of moral indignation turned inward

narrative:
"Certainly not."

"Suppose that a man thinks he has done a wrong to another, the nobler he is the less able is he to feel indignant at any suffering, such as hunger, or cold, or any other pain which the injured person may inflict upon him -- these he deems to be just, and, as I say, his anger refuses to be excited by them."
refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Consider this: when a person believes they have wronged another, the nobler they are, the less able they are to feel indignant at any suffering the wronged person inflicts on them -- hunger, cold, or any other pain. They consider the suffering just, and their anger refuses to rise against it."
elegant: A noble person who knows they've done wrong cannot feel indignant at the suffering inflicted by the wronged party. Their spirit accepts the punishment as just and refuses to be aroused.
summary: When a noble person recognizes they have wronged someone, they cannot feel indignant at any retaliatory suffering. They accept hunger, cold, and pain as just consequences, and their spirit refuses to rebel against deserved punishment.
distilled: A noble person's spirit accepts suffering inflicted by someone they have wronged, recognizing it as just and refusing to feel indignant.
notes: Speakers: Socrates to Glaucon -- This shows spirit (thymos) as responsive to justice: it flares when one is wronged but subsides when one has done wrong -- Spirit is thus a moral faculty, not merely emotional -- Cross-ref: 4.55 completes the picture with the opposite case -- The nobler the person, the more responsive their spirit is to moral truth -- This connects spirit to the virtue of courage (preservation of right opinion about what to fear) -- Cross-ref: Crito 49a-e where Socrates argues one should never return wrong for wrong -- The example shows that spirit is educated by reason's judgments about justice

narrative:
"True," he said.

"But when he thinks that he is the sufferer of the wrong, then he boils and chafes, and is on the side of what he believes to be justice; and because he suffers hunger or cold or other pain he is only the more determined to persevere and conquer. His noble spirit will not be quelled until he either slays or is slain; or until he hears the voice of the shepherd, that is, reason, bidding his dog bark no more."
refashioned:
"True."

"But when someone believes they are the one being wronged, they boil with indignation, siding with what they believe to be justice. Hunger, cold, and pain only make them more determined to persevere and prevail. Their noble spirit refuses to be quelled until they either conquer or die -- unless they hear the voice of the shepherd, that is, reason, telling its dog to stop barking."
elegant: When wronged, spirit boils with indignation, siding with justice. Suffering only strengthens its resolve. It fights until victory or death -- unless reason, like a shepherd, calls its dog to heel.
summary: When someone believes they've been wronged, their spirit boils with indignation, siding with justice. Suffering only strengthens their determination. Spirit fights to the death unless reason -- like a shepherd commanding its dog -- calls it to heel.
distilled: Spirit fights relentlessly for justice when one is wronged, yielding only to the calming voice of reason -- the shepherd commanding its dog.
notes: Speakers: Socrates to Glaucon -- The shepherd-dog metaphor is powerful and precise: reason is the shepherd, spirit is the dog -- Cross-ref: Book III 375a-376c where the guardian's nature is compared to a well-bred dog -- 'Slays or is slain' recalls Homeric warrior values -- The image of spirit 'boiling' (zeo) uses the same heat metaphor found in Homer's descriptions of anger -- Cross-ref: Iliad where Achilles' wrath is the paradigmatic case of thymos in action -- Spirit responds to perceived justice/injustice, confirming it as a moral faculty distinct from appetite -- The contrast between 4.54 (spirit quiescent when one has done wrong) and 4.55 (spirit aroused when one is wronged) demonstrates spirit's moral sensitivity

narrative:
"The illustration is perfect," he replied; "and in our State, as we were saying, the auxiliaries were to be dogs, and to hear the voice of the rulers, who are their shepherds."

"I perceive," I said, "that you quite understand me; there is, however, a further point which I wish you to consider."

"What point?"

"You remember that passion or spirit appeared at first sight to be a kind of desire, but now we should say quite the contrary; for in the conflict of the soul spirit is arrayed on the side of the rational principle."
refashioned:
"Perfect illustration," he replied. "And in our city, the auxiliaries were to be like dogs, obeying the voice of the rulers, their shepherds."

"You understand me well. But there's a further point to consider."

"What's that?"

"Remember that at first, spirit appeared to be a kind of desire. But now we should say the opposite: in the soul's inner conflicts, spirit always lines up on the side of reason."
elegant: The auxiliaries-as-dogs hearing the shepherd-rulers confirms the soul-city parallel. Spirit initially seemed akin to desire, but the evidence shows the opposite: in the soul's conflicts, spirit always sides with reason.
summary: Glaucon connects the shepherd-dog metaphor to the city: the auxiliaries are dogs obeying the ruler-shepherds. Socrates then highlights the reversal: spirit initially appeared to be a kind of desire, but in reality it always sides with reason in the soul's inner conflicts.
distilled: Spirit initially seemed akin to desire but proves to be the opposite -- it consistently allies with reason against appetite in the soul's inner conflicts.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The connection between the soul's structure and the city's is made fully explicit: auxiliaries (dogs/spirit) obey guardians (shepherds/reason) -- Cross-ref: Book III 375a-376c on the dog-like nature of guardians -- The reversal of Glaucon's initial assumption (4.52: 'akin to desire') is now complete -- Spirit is not appetite's ally but reason's -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 253c-e where the noble horse (spirit) assists the charioteer (reason) in controlling the base horse (appetite) -- This establishes the fundamental alliance structure of the well-ordered soul: reason rules, spirit enforces, appetite obeys

narrative:
"Most assuredly."

"But a further question arises: Is passion different from reason also, or only a kind of reason; in which latter case, instead of three principles in the soul, there will only be two, the rational and the concupiscent; or rather, as the State was composed of three classes, traders, auxiliaries, counsellors, so may there not be in the individual soul a third element which is passion or spirit, and when not corrupted by bad education is the natural auxiliary of reason?"
refashioned:
"Absolutely."

"But another question follows: is spirit actually different from reason, or is it just a form of reason? If it's a form of reason, the soul would have only two parts -- rational and appetitive. But just as the city has three classes -- traders, auxiliaries, and counselors -- might there not be a third element in the soul: spirit, which when not corrupted by bad education is reason's natural ally?"
elegant: Is spirit distinct from reason, or merely a form of it? If a form of reason, the soul has only two parts. But if spirit is a genuine third element -- as auxiliaries are a third class alongside traders and counselors -- then when not corrupted by bad education, it serves as reason's natural auxiliary.
summary: Socrates raises the final question: is spirit different from reason, or just a form of it? If the latter, the soul has only two parts. But paralleling the city's three classes (traders, auxiliaries, counselors), spirit may be a genuine third element that naturally serves as reason's ally when properly educated.
distilled: Spirit must be proven distinct from reason as well as from appetite; if it is, the soul has three parts mirroring the city's three classes.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the crucial step: having shown spirit is not appetite, Socrates must now show it's not reason either -- The three city classes (traders/producers, auxiliaries/warriors, counselors/guardians) are explicitly mapped to the soul -- 'When not corrupted by bad education' is an important qualification: spirit can be corrupted and fail to ally with reason -- Cross-ref: Book VIII-IX where degenerate souls show corrupted spirit -- 'Concupiscent' (epithymetikon) from Latin translation became the standard term -- The tripartite structure (reason, spirit, appetite / counselors, auxiliaries, traders) is the Republic's foundational psychological and political architecture

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "there must be a third."

"Yes," I replied, "if passion, which has already been shown to be different from desire, turn out also to be different from reason."

"But that is easily proved: -- We may observe even in young children that they are full of spirit almost as soon as they are born, whereas some of them never seem to attain to the use of reason, and most of them late enough."

"Excellent," I said, "and you may see passion equally in brute animals, which is a further proof of the truth of what you are saying. And we may once more appeal to the words of Homer, which have been already quoted by us,

'He smote his breast, and thus rebuked his soul,'

for in this verse Homer has clearly supposed the power which reasons about the better and worse to be different from the unreasoning anger which is rebuked by it."

"Very true," he said.

"And so, after much tossing, we have reached land, and are fairly agreed that the same principles which exist in the State exist also in the individual, and that they are three in number."

"Exactly."

"Must we not then infer that the individual is wise in the same way, and in virtue of the same quality which makes the State wise?"

"Certainly."

"Also that the same quality which constitutes courage in the State constitutes courage in the individual, and that both the State and the individual bear the same relation to all the other virtues?"

"Assuredly."

"And the individual will be acknowledged by us to be just in the same way in which the State is just?"

"That follows, of course."

"We cannot but remember that the justice of the State consisted in each of the three classes doing the work of its own class?"

"We are not very likely to have forgotten," he said.

"We must recollect that the individual in whom the several qualities of his nature do their own work will be just, and will do his own work?"

"Yes," he said, "we must remember that too."

"And ought not the rational principle, which is wise, and has the care of the whole soul, to rule, and the passionate or spirited principle to be the subject and ally?"
refashioned:
"Yes, there must be a third."

"There must -- provided spirit, already shown to be different from appetite, also proves different from reason."

"That's easily demonstrated. Young children are full of spirit almost from birth, yet some never develop reason at all, and most develop it quite late."

"Excellent point. And you can observe spirit in animals too, which lack reason entirely. We can also cite Homer again:

'He smote his breast, and thus rebuked his soul' --

where Homer clearly treats the reasoning power that deliberates about better and worse as different from the unreasoning anger it rebukes."

"Very true."

"So after a long voyage, we've reached land. We agree that the same three principles exist in the individual as in the city."

"Exactly."

"Then the individual must be wise in the same way and by the same quality that makes the city wise?"

"Certainly."

"And the same quality that constitutes courage in the city constitutes courage in the individual, and both bear the same relation to all the other virtues?"

"Absolutely."

"And the individual will be just in the same way the city is just?"

"That follows."

"And the city's justice consisted in each of its three classes doing its own work."

"We're not likely to have forgotten that."

"So the individual whose internal parts each do their own work will be just."

"We must remember that too."

"And shouldn't the rational part, which is wise and cares for the whole soul, rule -- with the spirited part as its subject and ally?"
elegant: Spirit is distinct from reason: children and animals have spirit without reason. Homer confirms this -- 'He smote his breast and rebuked his soul' shows reason chastising unreasoning anger. So three principles exist in both city and individual. The individual is wise, courageous, temperate, and just in exactly the same way as the city. The rational part should rule, with spirit as its ally, just as guardians rule with auxiliaries.
summary: Spirit is proven distinct from reason by two observations: children and animals have spirit without reason. Homer's line -- 'He smote his breast and rebuked his soul' -- confirms that reason and anger are separate powers. With three principles established in both city and individual, the virtues transfer: the individual is wise, courageous, and just in the same way as the city. Reason should rule the soul with spirit as its ally.
distilled: Children's early spirit and animals' spiritedness prove spirit is distinct from reason; with three soul-parts established, individual virtue mirrors civic virtue -- reason rules, spirit allies, appetite obeys.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon, with Glaucon providing key arguments -- The developmental argument (children have spirit before reason) and the animal argument (beasts have spirit without reason) are elegantly simple proofs -- Homer quotation is from Odyssey XX.17, where Odysseus restrains his anger at the suitors -- Cross-ref: This Homer passage was introduced in Book III -- 'After much tossing, we have reached land' is a nautical metaphor for philosophical arrival at truth -- The rapid transfer of all four virtues from city to individual completes the Republic's central argument -- Cross-ref: The city-soul analogy, first proposed in Book II, is now fully realized -- The rational principle 'has the care of the whole soul' parallels the guardians' care for the whole city

narrative:
"Certainly."

"And, as we were saying, the united influence of music and gymnastic will bring them into accord, nerving and sustaining the reason with noble words and lessons, and moderating and soothing and civilizing the wildness of passion by harmony and rhythm?"

"Quite true," he said.

"And these two, thus nurtured and educated, and having learned truly to know their own functions, will rule over the concupiscent, which in each of us is the largest part of the soul and by nature most insatiable of gain; over this they will keep guard, lest, waxing great and strong with the fulness of bodily pleasures, as they are termed, the concupiscent soul, no longer confined to her own sphere, should attempt to enslave and rule those who are not her natural-born subjects, and overturn the whole life of man?"
refashioned:
"Certainly."

"And as we said, the combined influence of music and gymnastic will bring reason and spirit into harmony -- strengthening and sustaining reason with noble ideas and lessons, while moderating, soothing, and civilizing the wildness of spirit through harmony and rhythm."

"True."

"These two, properly nurtured and educated to understand their functions, will govern the appetitive part -- which is the largest part of the soul and by nature the most insatiable in its cravings. They must keep guard over it, because if appetite grows strong and swollen on bodily pleasures, it will refuse to stay in its proper sphere and try to enslave and rule over the parts it has no right to govern -- overturning a person's entire life."
elegant: Music and gymnastic bring reason and spirit into harmony: music strengthens reason with noble ideas, while rhythm and harmony civilize spirit's wildness. Together, these two educated parts govern appetite -- the soul's largest and most insatiable element. They must prevent appetite from swelling with bodily pleasure and attempting to enslave the other parts, overturning the whole life.
summary: Music strengthens reason, harmony civilizes spirit, and together these two properly educated parts govern appetite -- the soul's largest and most insatiable element. They must prevent appetite from growing powerful on bodily pleasures and attempting to enslave the other parts, which would overturn a person's entire life.
distilled: Reason and spirit, harmonized by music and gymnastic, must jointly govern the soul's largest part -- appetite -- preventing it from swelling with pleasure and overturning the whole life.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Music nurtures reason, gymnastic tempers spirit -- this maps to the education described in Books II-III -- The appetitive soul as 'largest part and most insatiable' reflects Plato's view that desire is the dominant force in most people -- Cross-ref: Book IX 580d-581a where the three types of people are defined by which soul-part dominates -- 'No longer confined to her own sphere' describes the psychological version of injustice (class-meddling) -- The personification of appetite as female attempting to 'enslave and rule' uses political language for psychological dynamics -- Cross-ref: Book VIII-IX's portraits of the oligarchic, democratic, and tyrannical souls where appetite progressively takes over

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Both together will they not be the best defenders of the whole soul and the whole body against attacks from without; the one counselling, and the other fighting under his leader, and courageously executing his commands and counsels?"

"True."

"And he is to be deemed courageous whose spirit retains in pleasure and in pain the commands of reason about what he ought or ought not to fear?"

"Right," he replied.

"And him we call wise who has in him that little part which rules, and which proclaims these commands; that part too being supposed to have a knowledge of what is for the interest of each of the three parts and of the whole?"

"Assuredly."

"And would you not say that he is temperate who has these same elements in friendly harmony, in whom the one ruling principle of reason, and the two subject ones of spirit and desire are equally agreed that reason ought to rule, and do not rebel?"

"Certainly," he said, "that is the true account of temperance whether in the State or individual."

"And surely," I said, "we have explained again and again how and by virtue of what quality a man will be just."

"That is very certain."

"And is justice dimmer in the individual, and is her form different, or is she the same which we found her to be in the State?"

"There is no difference in my opinion," he said.

"Because, if any doubt is still lingering in our minds, a few commonplace instances will satisfy us of the truth of what I am saying."

"What sort of instances do you mean?"

"If the case is put to us, must we not admit that the just State, or the man who is trained in the principles of such a State, will be less likely than the unjust to make away with a deposit of gold or silver? Would any one deny this?"

"No one," he replied.

"Will the just man or citizen ever be guilty of sacrilege or theft, or treachery either to his friends or to his country?"
refashioned:
"True."

"Together, reason and spirit will be the best defenders of the whole soul and body against external threats -- one counseling, the other fighting under its leader's command and courageously executing orders."

"True."

"A person is courageous when their spirit retains, through pleasure and pain alike, reason's commands about what should and shouldn't be feared."

"Right."

"A person is wise when that small ruling part -- reason -- governs and issues commands, possessing knowledge of what benefits each of the three parts and the whole."

"Agreed."

"And a person is temperate when all three elements exist in friendly harmony -- when reason rules, and both spirit and desire agree that reason should rule, without rebelling."

"That is the true account of temperance, in the city or the individual."

"And we've explained repeatedly how a person becomes just."

"We certainly have."

"Is justice any dimmer in the individual than in the city? Is its form different?"

"No different, in my opinion."

"If any doubt remains, some simple examples should settle it."

"Such as?"

"Would the just person -- or someone raised in a just city's principles -- be less likely than the unjust to steal a deposit of gold or silver?"

"No one would deny that."

"Would the just person ever commit sacrilege, theft, or treachery against friends or country?"
elegant: Reason and spirit together defend the soul: reason counsels, spirit fights under its command. Courage is spirit retaining reason's commands through pleasure and pain. Wisdom is reason ruling with knowledge of each part's interest. Temperance is all three parts in harmony, agreeing reason should rule. Justice is all parts doing their own work. The just individual, like the just city, will not steal, commit sacrilege, or betray friends or country.
summary: The four virtues are now fully defined in the individual: courage is spirit preserving reason's commands; wisdom is reason ruling with knowledge of each part's interest; temperance is all parts harmoniously agreeing reason should rule; justice is each part doing its own work. Practical proof: the just person will not steal, commit sacrilege, or betray friends or country.
distilled: In the just individual, reason rules wisely, spirit enforces courageously, all parts agree temperately, and each performs its function -- producing a person who will never steal, betray, or commit sacrilege.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage completes the transfer of all four virtues from city to individual soul -- The practical test (would the just person steal a deposit?) connects abstract theory to concrete behavior -- Cross-ref: Book I where Cephalus defined justice as returning what is owed -- The 'deposit of gold or silver' (parakatatheke) was a standard example in Greek moral philosophy -- Cross-ref: Herodotus's story of Glaucus and the deposit (VI.86) -- Temperance as 'friendly harmony' among the three parts, with all agreeing reason should rule, is the most beautiful description of the well-ordered soul -- The question about sacrilege, theft, and treachery tests justice against the major Greek moral categories

narrative:
"Never."

"Neither will he ever break faith where there have been oaths or agreements?"

"Impossible."

"No one will be less likely to commit adultery, or to dishonour his father and mother, or to fall in his religious duties?"

"No one."

"And the reason is that each part of him is doing its own business, whether in ruling or being ruled?"

"Exactly so."

"Are you satisfied then that the quality which makes such men and such states is justice, or do you hope to discover some other?"

"Not I, indeed."

"Then our dream has been realised; and the suspicion which we entertained at the beginning of our work of construction, that some divine power must have conducted us to a primary form of justice, has now been verified?"

"Yes, certainly."

"And the division of labour which required the carpenter and the shoemaker and the rest of the citizens to be doing each his own business, and not another's, was a shadow of justice, and for that reason it was of use?"
refashioned:
"Never."

"Will he break faith where oaths or agreements have been made?"

"Impossible."

"Will anyone be less likely to commit adultery, dishonor their parents, or neglect their religious duties?"

"No one."

"And the reason for all this is that each part of their soul does its own work, whether ruling or being ruled."

"Exactly."

"Are you satisfied that the quality producing such people and such cities is justice? Or do you expect to find something else?"

"No, I'm satisfied."

"Then our dream has been realized. The suspicion we had at the beginning -- that some divine power was guiding us toward a primary form of justice -- has been confirmed."

"Yes."

"And the division of labor that required carpenter, shoemaker, and every citizen to do their own work and not another's -- that was a shadow of justice, and useful for that very reason."
elegant: The just person never breaks oaths, commits adultery, dishonors parents, or neglects religious duties -- because each part of their soul does its own work. The dream is realized: they have found justice. The division of labor in the city was a shadow of this deeper justice, useful precisely because it approximated the truth.
summary: The just person never breaks faith, commits adultery, dishonors parents, or neglects religious duties -- all because each soul-part performs its proper function. The quest is complete: justice has been found. The division of labor established earlier was a useful shadow of true justice, which is fundamentally about internal order, not just external role-assignment.
distilled: Justice in the soul -- each part doing its own work -- produces every practical virtue; the city's division of labor was merely a shadow of this deeper psychological truth.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The list of just behaviors (keeping oaths, honoring parents, religious duties) covers the major Greek moral obligations -- 'Shadow of justice' (eidolon dikaiosynes) uses the language that will become central to the cave allegory in Book VII -- The division of labor in the city was not justice itself but its image -- the real justice is internal -- Cross-ref: Book VII's shadows on the cave wall as images of higher realities -- 'Some divine power must have conducted us' suggests philosophical discovery is divinely guided -- Cross-ref: The Meno on recollection (anamnesis) as discovery guided by the divine -- The dream metaphor connects to Socrates' frequent references to waking from illusion to truth

narrative:
"Clearly."

"But in reality justice was such as we were describing, being concerned however, not with the outward man, but with the inward, which is the true self and concernment of man: for the just man does not permit the several elements within him to interfere with one another, or any of them to do the work of others -- he sets in order his own inner life, and is his own master and his own law, and at peace with himself; and when he has bound together the three principles within him, which may be compared to the higher, lower, and middle notes of the scale, and the intermediate intervals -- when he has bound all these together, and is no longer many, but has become one entirely temperate and perfectly adjusted nature, then he proceeds to act, if he has to act, whether in a matter of property, or in the treatment of the body, or in some affair of politics or private business; always thinking and calling that which preserves and co-operates with this harmonious condition, just and good action, and the knowledge which presides over it, wisdom, and that which at any time impairs this condition, he will call unjust action, and the opinion which presides over it ignorance."

"You have said the exact truth, Socrates."

"Very good; and if we were to affirm that we had discovered the just man and the just State, and the nature of justice in each of them, we should not be telling a falsehood?"
refashioned:
"Clearly."

"But true justice concerns not the outward person but the inward -- the true self. The just person does not allow the elements within to interfere with one another or do each other's work. They set their inner life in order, becoming their own master and their own law, at peace with themselves. When they have bound together the three principles within -- comparable to the high, low, and middle notes of a musical scale, with all the intervals between -- when these are bound into unity, and the person is no longer many but has become one entirely temperate, perfectly harmonized nature, then they act. Whether in matters of property, physical health, politics, or private affairs, they call 'just and good' whatever preserves this inner harmony, and 'wisdom' the knowledge that guides it. Whatever impairs this harmony they call 'unjust,' and the opinion behind it, 'ignorance.'"

"You have spoken the exact truth, Socrates."

"If we claimed to have discovered the just person, the just city, and the nature of justice in each, we would not be lying."
elegant: True justice is internal, not external. The just person sets their inner life in order, forbidding the soul's parts from interfering with one another. They bind the three principles together -- like high, low, and middle notes of a scale -- becoming one harmonized nature rather than many. Then every action that preserves this harmony is called just; whatever impairs it, unjust. The knowledge guiding harmony is wisdom; the opinion guiding discord is ignorance.
summary: Justice concerns the inner person, not the outer. The just person orders their soul so no part interferes with another, binding reason, spirit, and appetite together like the notes of a musical scale into one harmonized nature. Every action preserving this inner harmony is just and wise; every action disrupting it is unjust and ignorant. The just person and the just city have been discovered.
distilled: True justice is inner harmony -- the soul's three parts bound together like musical notes, producing a unified nature where every action preserves internal order.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is arguably the most important passage in the Republic -- the definitive statement of justice as inner harmony -- 'Not the outward man, but the inward' distinguishes Plato's justice from conventional Greek justice (which focused on external actions) -- 'His own master and his own law' (autos hautou archon) echoes the self-mastery discussion of 4.26-4.28 -- The musical scale analogy (high, low, middle notes plus intervals) creates a rich image of the harmonized soul -- 'No longer many, but has become one' fulfills the unity theme from 4.9-4.10 -- Cross-ref: Pythagorean musical-mathematical philosophy deeply influences this passage -- The definitions of just action (preserving harmony) and unjust action (impairing it) are beautifully simple -- Cross-ref: This is Plato's answer to Thrasymachus (Book I) and to Glaucon's challenge (Book II)

narrative:
"Most certainly not."

"May we say so, then?"

"Let us say so."

"And now," I said, "injustice has to be considered."

"Clearly."

"Must not injustice be a strife which arises among the three principles -- a meddlesomeness, and interference, and rising up of a part of the soul against the whole, an assertion of unlawful authority, which is made by a rebellious subject against a true prince, of whom he is the natural vassal -- what is all this confusion and delusion but injustice, and intemperance and cowardice and ignorance, and every form of vice?"
refashioned:
"Certainly not a falsehood."

"Then let us say so."

"Now we must consider injustice."

"Clearly."

"Injustice must be the conflict that arises among the three principles -- the meddling and interference, the rebellion of one part of the soul against the whole, the assertion of unlawful authority by a part that is naturally subordinate against the part that is its natural ruler. What is all this confusion and disorder but injustice itself? And is it not also intemperance, cowardice, ignorance -- every form of vice?"
elegant: Injustice is strife among the soul's three parts: meddling, interference, and the rebellion of a subordinate part against its natural ruler. This inner disorder is not only injustice but simultaneously intemperance, cowardice, ignorance -- every form of vice.
summary: Injustice is defined as internal strife: the soul's parts meddling with each other, a subordinate part rebelling against its natural ruler and asserting unlawful authority over the whole. This disorder is simultaneously injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance -- all vices are forms of inner disharmony.
distilled: Injustice is the rebellion of a subordinate soul-part against the whole, and this single disorder encompasses every vice -- intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance alike.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Political language describes psychological disorder: 'rebellious subject against a true prince,' 'unlawful authority,' 'natural vassal' -- This makes injustice a kind of inner tyranny or civil war -- Cross-ref: Book IX's portrait of the tyrannical soul as the ultimate case of inner rebellion -- The claim that all vices are forms of one disorder (injustice as inner disharmony) is a powerful unifying thesis -- Cross-ref: The Protagoras on the unity of virtue -- each vice corresponds to one of the four virtues failing: intemperance (loss of temperance), cowardice (loss of courage), ignorance (loss of wisdom), injustice (loss of justice) -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's sequence of degenerate constitutions mirrors the progressive breakdown of inner order

narrative:
"Exactly so."

"And if the nature of justice and injustice be known, then the meaning of acting unjustly and being unjust, or, again, of acting justly, will also be perfectly clear?"

"What do you mean?" he said.

"Why," I said, "they are like disease and health; being in the soul just what disease and health are in the body."

"How so?" he said.

"Why," I said, "that which is healthy causes health, and that which is unhealthy causes disease."

"Yes."

"And just actions cause justice, and unjust actions cause injustice?"

"That is certain."

"And the creation of health is the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the body; and the creation of disease is the production of a state of things at variance with this natural order?"

"True."

"And is not the creation of justice the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the soul, and the creation of injustice the production of a state of things at variance with the natural order?"

"Exactly so," he said.

"Then virtue is the health and beauty and well-being of the soul, and vice the disease and weakness and deformity of the same?"

"True."

"And do not good practices lead to virtue, and evil practices to vice?"

"Assuredly."

"Still our old question of the comparative advantage of justice and injustice has not been answered: Which is the more profitable, to be just and act justly and practise virtue, whether seen or unseen of gods and men, or to be unjust and act unjustly, if only unpunished and unreformed?"
refashioned:
"Exactly."

"So if we understand the nature of justice and injustice, the meaning of acting justly or unjustly becomes clear."

"How so?"

"Justice and injustice are to the soul what health and disease are to the body."

"Explain."

"What is healthy produces health; what is unhealthy produces disease."

"Yes."

"Just actions produce justice in the soul; unjust actions produce injustice."

"Certainly."

"Health is the establishment of natural order among the body's parts, where each governs and is governed according to nature. Disease is a state at variance with this natural order."

"True."

"Then justice is the establishment of natural order among the soul's parts, and injustice is disorder at variance with that natural order."

"Exactly."

"So virtue is the health, beauty, and well-being of the soul, and vice is its disease, weakness, and deformity."

"True."

"And good practices lead to virtue, evil practices to vice."

"Certainly."

"But our original question remains unanswered: which is more profitable -- to be just and practice virtue, whether or not gods and men observe it, or to be unjust and act unjustly, so long as one escapes punishment and correction?"
elegant: Justice is to the soul what health is to the body: the natural order of parts governing and being governed according to nature. Virtue is the soul's health, beauty, and well-being; vice is its disease, weakness, and deformity. Good practices produce virtue; evil practices produce vice. The original question returns: which is more profitable -- justice observed or unobserved, or unpunished injustice?
summary: Socrates draws the analogy between justice and health: both are the natural ordering of parts, where each element governs and is governed according to nature. Virtue is the soul's health and beauty; vice is its disease and deformity. This brings back the original question: which is more profitable -- practicing justice whether or not anyone sees it, or practicing injustice if one can escape punishment?
distilled: Justice is the soul's health -- natural order among its parts; vice is its disease -- and the question remains whether justice or unpunished injustice is more profitable.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The health-disease analogy for justice-injustice is one of Plato's most powerful arguments -- it makes the question of justice's profitability seem absurd, since no one would ask whether health is profitable -- Cross-ref: Book III 405a-410a on medicine and the healthy city -- The phrase 'whether seen or unseen of gods and men' directly recalls Glaucon's challenge in Book II (360b-362c) about the Ring of Gyges -- Cross-ref: The Ring of Gyges asked whether a person invisible to gods and men would still choose justice -- The answer is now obvious: who would choose to have a diseased soul? -- 'Unpunished and unreformed' (me didontos diken) adds the condition that makes injustice seem maximally attractive -- This sets up the final reductio in 4.65

narrative: "In my judgment, Socrates, the question has now become ridiculous. We know that, when the bodily constitution is gone, life is no longer endurable, though pampered with all kinds of meats and drinks, and having all wealth and all power; and shall we be told that when the very essence of the vital principle is undermined and corrupted, life is still worth having to a man, if only he be allowed to do whatever he likes with the single exception that he is not to acquire justice and virtue, or to escape from injustice and vice; assuming them both to be such as we have described?"
refashioned: "In my judgment, Socrates, the question is now ridiculous. We know that when the body's constitution is ruined, life becomes unendurable -- no matter how much food, drink, wealth, and power you have. So are we really supposed to believe that when the soul's vital principle is undermined and corrupted, life is still worth living -- as long as the person can do whatever they want, with the sole restriction that they cannot acquire justice and virtue or escape from injustice and vice? Given what we've now shown justice and injustice to be, the question answers itself."
elegant: The question is now absurd. When bodily health is destroyed, no amount of wealth or pleasure makes life endurable. How then could life be worth living when the soul's very essence is corrupted -- when one possesses everything except justice and virtue?
summary: Glaucon declares the question of justice's profitability has become ridiculous. Just as no amount of wealth or pleasure makes life worth living when the body's health is destroyed, life cannot be worth living when the soul's essential principle -- justice -- is corrupted. The health analogy has made the answer self-evident.
distilled: The question of whether injustice can be profitable is now absurd -- just as no one would choose bodily ruin for any amount of wealth, no one should choose the corruption of their soul.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon delivers the judgment, confirming Socrates' argument -- This is the dramatic payoff of the entire Republic so far -- Glaucon, who posed the challenge in Book II (that injustice might be more profitable), now declares the question 'ridiculous' -- The body-soul parallel is complete: health of body :: justice of soul, therefore justice is obviously preferable -- 'All kinds of meats and drinks, all wealth and all power' represents the maximum imaginable external goods -- even these cannot compensate for inner corruption -- Cross-ref: Book II's Ring of Gyges and the invisible unjust man -- the answer is now clear: even invisible, the unjust person is miserable because their soul is diseased -- Cross-ref: Book IX will further quantify the superiority of the just life

narrative:
"Yes," I said, "the question is, as you say, ridiculous. Still, as we are near the spot at which we may see the truth in the clearest manner with our own eyes, let us not faint by the way."

"Certainly not," he replied.

"Come up hither," I said, "and behold the various forms of vice, those of them, I mean, which are worth looking at."

"I am following you," he replied: "proceed."

"I said, The argument seems to have reached a height from which, as from some tower of speculation, a man may look down and see that virtue is one, but that the forms of vice are innumerable; there being four special ones which are deserving of note."

"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean," I replied, "that there appear to be as many forms of the soul as there are distinct forms of the State."

"How many?"

"There are five of the State, and five of the soul," I said.

"What are they?"

"The first," I said, "is that which we have been describing, and which may be said to have two names, monarchy and aristocracy, accordingly as rule is exercised by one distinguished man or by many."

"True," he replied.

"But I regard the two names as describing one form only; for whether the government is in the hands of one or many, if the governors have been trained in the manner which we have supposed, the fundamental laws of the State will be maintained."

"That is true," he replied.
refashioned:
"Yes, the question is ridiculous. But since we're close to the point where we can see the truth most clearly, let's not give up now."

"Certainly not."

"Come higher, then, and look at the various forms of vice -- at least the ones worth examining."

"I'm following. Go on."

"From this height in our argument -- as from a tower of speculation -- we can look down and see that virtue is one, while the forms of vice are innumerable. Four in particular deserve attention."

"What do you mean?"

"There are as many forms of the soul as there are distinct forms of government."

"How many?"

"Five forms of government and five forms of the soul."

"What are they?"

"The first is the one we've been describing, which goes by two names: monarchy when one distinguished person rules, aristocracy when several do."

"True."

"But I regard these as one form, not two. Whether one or many govern, as long as the rulers are trained as we've described, the fundamental laws of the city will be maintained."

"That's true."
elegant: From the height of their argument, they can see that virtue is one while the forms of vice are innumerable -- four deserve special attention. There are five forms of government and five corresponding forms of the soul. The first and best -- the one they've been building -- is monarchy or aristocracy, which are really one form: so long as rulers are properly trained, the fundamental laws hold whether one rules or many.
summary: From the summit of their argument, Socrates sees that virtue is one while vice takes innumerable forms -- four especially worth examining. Five forms of government correspond to five forms of the soul. The first and best is what they've been constructing: monarchy or aristocracy, which are fundamentally one form, since properly trained rulers maintain the same laws whether governing alone or in a group. This sets up the analysis of degenerate constitutions in Books VIII-IX.
distilled: Virtue is one, vice innumerable; five forms of government match five forms of the soul, with the best being monarchy/aristocracy -- one form of just rule -- and four forms of degeneration to follow.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Tower of speculation' (skopia) is a memorable image for the philosophical vantage point -- 'Virtue is one, forms of vice innumerable' echoes Tolstoy's later observation about happy and unhappy families -- The five forms of government: aristocracy/monarchy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny -- these will be analyzed in Books VIII-IX -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Politics III develops a different classification of constitutions -- The five soul types correspond to the five constitutions: philosophical/kingly, honor-loving, money-loving, freedom-loving, tyrannical -- Monarchy and aristocracy as one form shows Plato values the quality of rulers over their number -- This passage serves as the bridge to Books VIII-IX, announcing the program of analyzing degenerate constitutions and souls -- Book IV ends here with the major arguments complete: the four virtues of the city, the tripartite soul, justice as inner harmony
